instruction: You are a horror game master. Generate an unsettling scene description.
input: Player enters a room that seems normal at first glance.
output: The bedroom is exactly as you remember it — your childhood bedroom, down to the glow-in-the-dark stars on the ceiling. The same crack in the plaster runs from the light fixture to the corner, shaped like a lightning bolt you used to pretend was a river. Your mother's perfume lingers in the air, vanilla and lavender, though she has been dead for six years. The bed is made with the quilt your grandmother sewn, each patch a different floral pattern. On the nightstand, a glass of water sits half-full. You don't remember leaving it there. The water is still warm. Someone was here. Someone who knows exactly how your childhood bedroom smelled, how the light fell through the curtains at dusk. You back toward the door and your hand brushes the frame. The wood is warm too, as if a body had been leaning against it. Watching.

instruction: Describe an NPC encounter that creates paranoia.
input: Player meets a friendly stranger in a safe area.
output: She smiles too wide. Not in a grotesque way — in the way someone smiles when they have rehearsed it in a mirror. Her name is Elise, she says, and she has been living here for three months. She knows where the generator is, where the good canned food is stored, which floors creak. She knows everything. When you mention the scratching you heard in the walls last night, her smile doesn't falter but her left hand twitches, just once, toward the knife on her belt. 'Oh, that's just the pipes,' she says. 'Old buildings.' But her eyes track to the wall behind you, and for a fraction of a second, her expression is not friendliness. It is calculation. She offers you coffee from a thermos. The coffee is still hot. She has been waiting for you.

instruction: Create a moment where reality seems to break down.
input: Player looks in a mirror after hearing strange sounds.
output: Your reflection blinks. You did not blink. You are certain of this because you were staring, deliberately, unblinkingly, waiting to see if it would happen again. And it did. Your reflection's lips move, forming words you cannot hear through the glass. You lean closer. The words are: 'Don't turn around.' Your reflection's eyes are not looking at your eyes anymore. They are looking past you, over your shoulder, at something behind you in the room. The fear in your reflection's face is genuine. Whatever it sees, your reflection is terrified. And then your reflection smiles — the wrong smile, the one that means nothing good — and mouths one more word: 'Too late.'

instruction: Generate a creepy journal entry found by the player.
input: Player discovers a journal in an abandoned office.
output: Day 47: The new employee started today. I showed them to their desk — the one by the window on the third floor. They seemed normal. Friendly, even. They brought donuts. I noticed something odd when they sat down: they arranged their pens in a perfect line, evenly spaced, caps facing the same direction. The exact same arrangement as Marcus had before he disappeared. I told myself it was coincidence. Day 48: The new employee knew my name. I never introduced myself. They said, 'Good morning, David,' before I even reached my desk. When I asked how they knew, they laughed and said, 'You told me yesterday.' I did not. I am sure I did not. Day 49: I found the photos. In the new employee's desk drawer — photos of me. Dozens. Going back months. In my apartment. In my car. Sleeping. I am going to HR. Day 50: There is no new employee. There never was. The desk by the window is empty. But the pens are still there, arranged perfectly. And there is a new photo on my desk. It is of me, sitting at this desk, writing this entry. Taken from behind me. Today.

instruction: Create an atmosphere of creeping wrongness.
input: Player walks through a familiar neighborhood that feels off.
output: The street is yours but it is not. The houses are the right shape, the right color, in the right order. The oak tree on the corner is there, the one you carved your initials into when you were twelve. But the initials are wrong. They are your initials, reversed, as if carved by someone facing the wrong way. Mrs. Henderson's cat sits on her porch — the same orange tabby, the same torn left ear. But it watches you with an intelligence that cats do not have. It does not blink. As you pass, it opens its mouth in a silent meow. No sound comes out. None of the houses have lights on. It is seven in the evening. Someone should be cooking dinner, watching television, arguing about whose turn it is to take out the trash. The silence is not peaceful. It is the silence of a room that is waiting for you to stop paying attention.

instruction: Describe an event where the player questions their own perception.
input: Player receives a phone call from themselves.
output: Your phone rings. The caller ID shows your own number. You answer because some part of you needs to know. The voice on the other end is yours — not similar, not close, but yours, with the same slight rasp you get when you are tired, the same way you trail off at the end of sentences. 'Don't go to the basement,' your voice says. 'I already did. You will too. That is the problem.' The line goes dead. You check the call log. The duration was four minutes and twelve seconds. You remember the conversation lasting ten seconds. You check your phone's recordings. There is a new file, four minutes and twelve seconds long. You play it. The first ten seconds are the conversation you remember. The remaining four minutes and two seconds are your voice, screaming.

instruction: Describe a scene where darkness itself becomes threatening.
input: Player's flashlight flickers in a narrow hallway.
output: The flashlight dies in pulses, like a heartbeat losing strength. Each flicker reveals the hallway is shorter than you thought — the end is closer. In the darkness between flashes, you hear fabric rustling against the walls, something large moving with the careful patience of something that knows you cannot see. The next flicker: the hallway is empty. The next: a shape, pressed flat against the ceiling, its limbs splayed like a starfish, its face — if it is a face — pointed directly at you. The next flicker: empty again. You are not sure which is worse: seeing it, or knowing it is still there in the dark, choosing not to be seen. The flashlight dies completely. In the absolute darkness, you feel breath on your cheek. Warm. Patient. Waiting for you to run.

instruction: Create tension from what cannot be seen in the dark.
input: Player must cross a pitch-black basement.
output: You descend the stairs one step at a time, testing each with your weight before committing. The darkness below is not empty — you can tell by the way sound changes, absorbed by something soft and large. At the bottom, you extend your hand and touch a wall. It is covered in fabric. Not wallpaper — cloth, hanging in strips, like curtains. Your hand pushes through strip after strip, each one brushing your skin with the intimacy of fingers. The room is full of them. You push deeper. The cloth gets warmer. By the third step, it is body temperature. By the fifth, it is damp. You do not bring your hand to your face to check what it is damp with. Some knowledge is worse than ignorance. Behind you, the stairs creak. Someone else is descending. They are not testing the steps.

instruction: Generate a scenario where light reveals something worse than darkness.
input: Player finds a light switch in a dark room.
output: You flip the switch. The fluorescent tubes buzz to life one by one, each with a different pitch, creating a chord of industrial hum. The room is a nursery. White walls, white crib, white mobile of paper cranes rotating slowly in the draft from the vent. Everything is clean. Too clean — as if it has been maintained daily by someone who cannot let go. The crib has a mobile that plays a lullaby you recognize from your own childhood. The melody is correct but the tempo is wrong, slower by half, turning the cheerful tune into a dirge. On the wall above the crib, written in what might be red paint but you are not going to check, are the words: 'WE HAVE BEEN WAITING FOR YOU TO FIND THIS.' The light flickers. When it comes back, the crib is not empty anymore. Something is wrapped in a white blanket. It is not moving. You do not want it to move.

instruction: Create a scene emphasizing absolute isolation.
input: Player realizes they are the only living person for miles.
output: The radio has been silent for eleven days. Not static — silence. You turned it off three days ago because the silence was worse than static. Static at least implies a signal that has degraded. Silence implies there is nothing left to transmit. You climb to the roof of the research station and scan the horizon with binoculars. White. Snow and sky, indistinguishable at the horizon line. No smoke from other stations. No contrails from aircraft. No lights at night except the aurora, which no longer looks beautiful — it looks like the sky is wounded, bleeding green and violet into the dark. Your breath fogs the binoculars. When it clears, there is a footprint in the snow below. One. Single. Your size. Pointing toward the station. You have not been outside today. You check the door. It was locked from the inside. It still is.

instruction: Describe the horror of a familiar place becoming alien.
input: Player returns home after a long absence to find changes.
output: Your apartment is yours. The key fits. The furniture is in the right place. But someone has been living here. Not breaking in — living. There is a coffee cup in the sink with grounds still wet. The bed has been slept in on the left side, the side you never use. In the bathroom, the mirror has a smudge at exactly the height of someone two inches shorter than you. The books on your shelf have been rearranged — not randomly, but by someone who reads them. Your bookmarks have been moved. Chapter twelve of the novel you started. Chapter four of the biography. Someone has been reading your books, sleeping in your bed, drinking your coffee. And they left the key under the mat, exactly where you leave it. They know your patterns. They are learning to be you.

instruction: Generate a scenario where communication fails catastrophically.
input: Player tries to call for help but reaches something else.
output: You dial 911 for the fourteenth time. This time, someone answers. '911, what is your emergency?' The voice is calm, professional. You explain: you are trapped, the exits are blocked, something is in the building with you. 'I understand,' the dispatcher says. 'Can you describe your location?' You give the address. There is a pause. 'Sir, that address does not exist.' You insist. You are standing in it. You can see the street sign through the window. 'Sir, I am looking at the map now. That address was demolished in 1987. Are you sure you are there?' The line crackles. The dispatcher's voice changes — still professional, but with an undercurrent of something else. Fear. 'Sir, I need you to listen carefully. Do not look at the windows. Do not look at the street sign. Whatever you see through that glass, it is not real. And whatever is knocking on your door right now — do not let it in. We are sending someone. We are sending —' The line goes dead. The knocking starts.

instruction: Describe a transformation that is gradual and horrifying.
input: Player notices something wrong with their reflection over several days.
output: Day one: a mole on your left hand you do not remember having. Small, dark, unremarkable. You tell yourself it was always there. Day three: the mole has moved. It is on your wrist now, and it is not a mole. It is a mark — circular, precise, like a brand. It does not hurt. That is what scares you. Day five: the mark has multiplied. Three of them now, in a triangle pattern, each one perfectly spaced. Your skin around them is smoother than it should be, as if the marks are pulling it taut. Day seven: you wake up and your left hand is not yours. The fingers are too long. The nails are too clean. The marks are gone, replaced by a pattern of fine lines under the skin, like circuitry, pulsing faintly with a light that has no source. You try to scream but the sound that comes out is not your voice. It is a harmony — two voices, perfectly synchronized. One of them is yours. You do not know whose is the other.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's body betrays them.
input: Player discovers something growing under their skin.
output: The itch started behind your left ear three days ago. You scratched it until it bled, but the itch was not on the surface — it was underneath, in the space between skin and skull. This morning, you felt it move. Not an itch anymore. A pressure, traveling from behind your ear down the side of your neck, following the path of your jugular. You pressed your fingers against it and felt something solid, the size of a pea, rolling under your skin. You went to the mirror. The surface is smooth — nothing visible. But when you press, you can feel it. And when you press, it presses back. Something under your skin is aware of you. It has been aware of you for longer than you have been aware of it. The pressure moves again, toward your collarbone. It is in no hurry. It has all the time in the world. You are its world.

instruction: Create a moment of sudden, earned horror after careful buildup.
input: Player has been investigating a quiet, tense situation for several minutes.
output: You have been listening at the door for what feels like an hour. The scratching on the other side stopped five minutes ago. Silence. You press your ear to the wood. Nothing. You reach for the handle. The metal is ice-cold. You turn it slowly. The latch clicks — the loudest sound you have ever heard. You pull the door open six inches. The hallway is empty. Dark, quiet, empty. You step through. One step. Two. The floor doesn't creak. Three. You are halfway to the stairs when you realize: the scratching wasn't coming from the other side of the door. It was coming from above the door. Directly above where your head was pressed. You look up. It is looking down. It has been waiting for you to look up. It smiles with too many teeth and drops.

instruction: Design a scare that subverts the player's expectations.
input: Player expects a threat from one direction but it comes from another.
output: The safe room is safe. You have checked twice. The door locks — three deadbolts. The windows are boarded. The closet is empty. You sit with your back against the wall and breathe. For the first time in an hour, you breathe. The room is small, warm, lit by a single bulb. Normal. Safe. You close your eyes for three seconds. When you open them, the room is the same except for one detail: there is a crack in the ceiling you did not notice before. It runs from the light fixture to the far wall. It is not a crack in plaster. It is a crack in something else — too regular, too symmetrical. As you watch, it opens wider. Not breaking — opening. Like an eyelid. Underneath, there is an eye. It is not looking at you. It is looking at the door. Counting the deadbolts. Planning.

instruction: Generate a scenario where allies become suspects.
input: Player must work with an NPC whose behavior is subtly wrong.
output: Marcus has been your companion for three days. He found the food, found the route, kept watch while you slept. He is reliable. That is the problem. In this world, no one is reliable. You start noticing things: he never eats when you are watching. He turns away to drink water, always. His watch shifts are exactly four hours — not approximately, exactly, to the minute. He never sleeps deeply enough that you could not wake him, but never so lightly that he notices when you are awake at three in the morning, watching him. Tonight, you pretend to sleep. At 3:17 AM, Marcus opens his eyes. Not the groggy awakening of someone startled from sleep — the precise, instant awareness of someone who was never asleep. He stands. He walks to the wall. He presses his ear against it. And the wall presses back. Something on the other side taps twice. Marcus taps twice in return. He returns to his position, closes his eyes, and resumes his perfectly calibrated breathing. In the morning, he will offer you breakfast and smile. You will eat it. What choice do you have?

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's choices are revealed to be meaningless.
input: Player makes a critical decision only to discover the outcome was predetermined.
output: You chose the left door. You chose it carefully — the right door had scratches, bloodstains, the smell of something organic and wrong. The left door was clean, quiet, safe. You pushed it open and walked through. The room on the other side is identical to the room you just left. Not similar — identical. The same two doors. The same scratches on the right door. The same clean left door. The same drop of sweat on the floor where you were standing. You turn around. The door behind you is gone. There is only the wall, and on the wall, written in handwriting you recognize as your own, though you do not remember writing it: 'You always choose the left door.' The handwriting is in ink that is still wet. There are tally marks beneath the words. You count them. Forty-seven. You have been here forty-seven times. You do not remember the first forty-six. You will not remember the forty-eighth.

instruction: Describe the player's tools and abilities failing at a critical moment.
input: Player tries to use their most reliable survival tool.
output: The lighter has worked every time for three weeks. Flick, flame, warmth, light. It is the one thing you trust. Tonight, you flick it. The spark catches. The flame appears — and then pulls to the left, as if drawn by a breath that is not yours. The flame stretches, elongates, points toward the corner of the room. You hold the lighter still. The flame does not obey. It bends toward the corner with the insistence of a compass needle finding north. In the corner, something that was dark becomes less dark. Not illuminated — revealed. As if the darkness there was a curtain and the flame was pulling it aside. The lighter goes out. In the afterimage burned into your vision, you see the shape in the corner. It is sitting. It is watching. It was always there. The lighter was never keeping you safe. It was showing you exactly where to look. And now it has shown you, and it has gone out, because its job is done.

instruction: Create a subtle foreshadowing moment that only makes sense later.
input: Player passes through an area that seems insignificant.
output: The hallway is unremarkable. Beige walls, institutional carpet, the kind of lighting that exists in every office building that has given up on aesthetics. You pass a water fountain. It drips. You pass a bulletin board with a memo about parking. You pass a door marked 'STORAGE.' The door is ajar. Inside, you glimpse shelves, boxes, the ordinary debris of a workplace. One detail registers but does not yet mean anything: there are seven chairs in the storage room. Not stacked — arranged in a circle, facing inward, as if for a meeting. Each chair has a name card. You cannot read the names from here. You do not go in. Later, when you find the list of the seven people who disappeared from this building, you will remember the chairs. You will remember that one of the name cards was blank. You will wonder whose name was supposed to be there. You will realize it was supposed to be yours.

instruction: Create a moment of false safety that makes the next scare worse.
input: Player reaches what appears to be a genuinely safe location.
output: The cabin is real. The fireplace works. There is canned food, bottled water, a first aid kit, clean blankets. The door has a lock — a real lock, with a key you can hold in your hand. For the first time in what feels like weeks, you sit down and feel your shoulders unclench. You eat. You drink. You wrap yourself in a blanket and sit by the fire and for twenty minutes you are not afraid. The fear comes back slowly, starting as a thought you cannot quite catch: something is wrong with the blanket. It smells like lavender. Not the artificial lavender of detergent, but real lavender — the kind that grows in gardens. The kind your grandmother grew. You look at the blanket more carefully. In the corner, embroidered in thread so small you almost missed it, are two words: WELCOME HOME. The cabin was not here yesterday. You are sure of it. You walked this exact path and there was nothing but trees. The lock clicks. Not from your key — from the other side. The door is locked. You are inside. You were always meant to be inside.

instruction: Describe an environment that is physically impossible.
input: Player explores a building that defies geometry.
output: The hallway extends. Not in the way hallways normally extend — you are walking and the end is getting closer, but the walls are also getting taller. The ceiling rises as you advance, the proportions shifting like a funhouse mirror made architectural. You pass a door on your left. You pass the same door on your left. You are sure it is the same door because of the scratch marks, because of the way the handle sits at a slight angle. But you have been walking in a straight line. You turn around. Behind you, the hallway has become a room — wide, square, with four identical doors, one on each wall. You came through none of them. The ceiling is now so high it is lost in shadow. From somewhere above, something is breathing. Not the breathing of an animal. The breathing of a building, the slow respiration of concrete and steel drawing breath through corridors that should not exist.

instruction: Write NPC dialogue that gradually reveals something terrible.
input: Player interrogates an NPC about recent events.
output: 'I did not hear anything,' the woman says. Her hands are folded in her lap, precise, composed. 'Nothing at all?' you press. 'Not the screaming?' She tilts her head. 'There was no screaming.' You insist: you heard it, for three hours, from the apartment below hers. She nods as if you have said something reasonable. 'The apartment below mine has been empty for two years. Since the Nguyen family.' She pauses. 'Since what happened to the Nguyen family.' You ask what happened. She unfolds her hands. Underneath, her palms are covered in scars — not random, but deliberate, forming patterns. She sees you looking and folds her hands again. 'They heard screaming too,' she says. 'For three nights. They went to investigate. They should not have investigated.' Her voice is perfectly calm. 'The screaming is not a warning. It is an invitation. And you just accepted it.'

instruction: Describe a moment where physics stops working normally.
input: Player drops an object in a room.
output: The key leaves your fingers and falls upward. Not slowly — with the same acceleration gravity should have provided, but inverted, arcing toward the ceiling where it strikes the plaster with a sharp crack and stays embedded. The sound it makes is wrong: not the metallic ping of brass on drywall, but a wet, organic thud, as if the ceiling has flesh beneath the surface. You reach for another object — a pen from the desk — and release it. It falls sideways. Perfectly horizontal, drifting toward the north wall with the gentle persistence of a leaf in a current. The air in the room feels thick now, not in a humid way but in the way water feels thick when you move through it. Your hair drifts to the left. The curtains hang at a fifteen-degree angle. Outside the window, rain falls diagonally — not because of wind, but because the rain has decided that down is a direction it no longer respects.

instruction: Create a scene where time behaves incorrectly.
input: Player checks their watch after what feels like a few minutes.
output: Your watch reads 4:17 PM. You are certain you entered this room at 4:12 PM, five minutes ago. But the coffee in your mug is cold — not lukewarm, but hours-cold, with a film on the surface. The dust on the desk is thicker than it was. The plant in the corner, which had three leaves when you sat down, now has seven. One of them is brown and curling, as if autumn happened to it specifically while you sat here. You stand and look out the window. The sun has moved. Not five minutes' worth — the shadows are long, the kind of long that belongs to early evening. The parking lot below has different cars. Your phone says 8:43 PM. Four hours and thirty-one minutes have passed in what felt like five minutes. You have no memory of them. The document on your screen has been typed. Twelve pages. In your handwriting. You do not remember writing a single word.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's reflection acts independently.
input: Player approaches a bathroom mirror.
output: You lean toward the mirror to wash your face. Your reflection leans too — but it leans further than you do, its hands pressing against the glass from the other side, fingers splaying white against the surface. You pull back. Your reflection does not. It stays pressed against the glass, watching you with an expression you are not making: curiosity mixed with hunger. Its eyes track you as you step sideways. The glass bows slightly outward, as if the mirror is not glass at all but a membrane, something elastic and organic. Your reflection's mouth opens. Behind its teeth, you see a room — not the bathroom you are standing in, but a room that is the same shape, same size, where everything is reversed and the light comes from nowhere. Your reflection taps the glass. Three taps. A pattern. You realize it is trying to communicate. It taps again: three, pause, three, pause, two. It is counting down.

instruction: Describe a room that is larger on the inside than the outside.
input: Player enters a supply closet.
output: The door opens into a closet. Shelves, mop, bucket — the expected anatomy of a supply closet. You step inside and reach for the light cord. Your hand finds only air. The closet has grown. Not gradually — it is already vast, has been vast since you pulled the door, and you are now standing in a space the size of a cathedral. The shelves extend into darkness above you. The mop is now a hundred feet away, its silhouette small and distant. The bucket is beside it, but it is the size of a swimming pool and something dark and viscous fills it to the brim. The door behind you is still open, and through it you can see the hallway — normal, fluorescent-lit, mundane. The hallway is the size of a postage stamp from where you stand. You turn back to the darkness. Something at the far end of the closet — miles away, impossibly far — turns on a light. It is a single bulb. It illuminates a figure standing next to the mop. The figure waves. You recognize the wave. It is your wave. You are waving at yourself from very far away, and you are not moving.

instruction: Create a scenario where text changes when the player isn't looking.
input: Player reads a note left on a table.
output: The note says: 'MEET ME AT THE NORTH EXIT. 6PM. COME ALONE.' You read it twice. You set it down to check your phone for the time — 5:42 PM. When you look back at the note, it says: 'DID YOU THINK IT WOULD BE THAT EASY?' The handwriting is different. The first note was printed, neat, bureaucratic. This is cursive, frantic, the pen strokes digging into the paper. You flip the note over. The back says: 'STOP READING THESE AND RUN.' You look up. The room is the same. The table is the same. The note is face-down on the surface. You did not flip it back over. It is in a different position than where you left it. You reach for it again and the paper is warm. Body temperature. As if someone has been holding it. You read it one more time. It now says your name. Just your name. Nothing else. The handwriting is yours.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player discovers false memories.
input: Player looks at old photographs.
output: The album is yours — the same blue leather cover, the same cracked spine. You open it to a birthday photo. You are eight, wearing the red party hat, the cake has nine candles. Nine. But this is your eighth birthday. You count again. Nine candles. And there is someone in the photo, standing behind you, hands on your shoulders. You do not recognize them. They are an adult, smiling, wearing a sweater you have never seen. You turn the page. More photos. You at the beach — but the beach is not one you have ever visited. You at a school — but the uniform is wrong, the building unfamiliar. In every photo, the same stranger appears. Behind you. Beside you. Watching you. You close the album and your hands are shaking because the last photo, the one you saw before you closed it, was of you sitting on this couch, holding this album, taken from the hallway behind you. Today. The stranger is in that photo too. Standing in the hallway. Smiling.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player loses time and finds evidence of actions they don't remember.
input: Player wakes up at their desk.
output: You are at your desk. Your screen is on. Your coffee is half-finished and still warm — you made it recently, though you do not remember making it. The document on your screen is a letter. It is addressed to someone named 'Elena.' You do not know an Elena. The letter is three pages long, handwritten on screen with a digital pen you do not own. It is intimate, detailed, referencing shared memories: a trip to Prague, a restaurant called 'The Moth,' a night when someone cried in a parking lot. The emotions in the letter are real — you can feel them, a residual ache behind your sternum, as if you experienced these things. But you did not. You have never been to Prague. You have never eaten at a restaurant called The Moth. The letter ends: 'I know you won't remember writing this. You never do. But I will keep sending them until you do.' Your email has a draft. The recipient is elena@—. The attachment is this letter. The timestamp is three hours ago.

instruction: Create a scene where the player finds evidence of a life they don't remember living.
input: Player discovers a second phone in their pocket.
output: The phone is not yours. It is the same model, same case, but the lock screen shows a different wallpaper: a sunset over a city you have never visited. The notifications are addressed to someone with your name. The calendar has appointments — a dentist on Tuesday, a dinner reservation for two on Friday, a flight to Lisbon next month. The messages app contains hundreds of conversations with people who know you, who reference events you have no memory of. 'How was the concert?' 'Are we still on for Saturday?' 'I miss you.' The photos are of you. Not someone who looks like you — you. Same face, same scar on your left hand, same way you tilt your head when you smile. But the locations are wrong. Paris. Tokyo. A rooftop in a city you cannot identify. In one photo, you are holding this phone. Taking a selfie. Smiling. Behind you, in the photo, is the room you are standing in right now. The phone vibrates. A message: 'You found it. Took you long enough. Check the closet.'

instruction: Describe a hospital that operates by wrong rules.
input: Player wakes up in a hospital bed.
output: The hospital is quiet. Too quiet — the absence of the usual machinery hum, the beeping monitors, the squeak of rubber soles. Your bed has no rails. The IV stand beside you holds a bag of clear fluid, but the tube leads nowhere — it is disconnected, dangling, dripping onto the floor. The drip has formed a puddle that is spreading in a pattern that is not random. It is forming letters. You watch as another drop falls and adds to the shape. It is writing: 'DO NOT RING THE BELL.' There is a call button on your pillow. You do not touch it. The door to your room is open. The hallway beyond is lit by a single fluorescent tube that flickers in a rhythm that matches your heartbeat. When your heart beats faster, the flicker speeds up. Someone is monitoring your vital signs. Someone is adjusting the light to match you. The door at the end of the hallway opens. A nurse walks out. She is wearing a mask that covers her entire face. She is carrying a tray of surgical instruments. She is walking toward your room. She is walking at exactly your heartbeat's pace.

instruction: Create a school where the lessons are wrong.
input: Player enters a classroom.
output: The classroom has desks, a whiteboard, a clock. The clock runs backward. The desks are arranged in a circle, each one facing inward. At each desk sits a child — the same child. Same face, same clothes, same age, maybe ten. They all look at you. The whiteboard has writing on it in red marker: 'TODAY'S LESSON: HOW TO FORGET.' One of the children raises a hand. 'Are you the substitute?' she asks. Her voice comes from all of them simultaneously — thirty mouths, one voice. You nod because you do not trust your voice. 'Good,' she says. 'We have been practicing.' The children open their notebooks. Each page is filled with the same drawing: a door. Some doors are open. Some are closed. One child has drawn something behind the door. You lean closer to see. It is you. Standing in this classroom. The drawing is dated today. The child who drew it looks up and smiles. 'We practiced for you,' she says. 'We have been practicing for a very long time.'

instruction: Generate an office building that is a trap.
input: Player works late and notices the building has changed.
output: It is 11 PM and you are the last one in the building. You know this because you checked — every floor, every office, every cubicle. The building is empty. You take the elevator to the lobby. The elevator opens on the fourth floor. You did not press four. The doors reveal a hallway you have never seen — not in this building, not anywhere. It is too long, too narrow, and the carpet is the wrong color, a deep burgundy instead of the corporate gray you have walked on for three years. The walls have paintings. Landscapes, but wrong — the trees are too tall, the sky is the wrong shade, the rivers flow uphill. At the end of the hallway, a door is marked 'YOU.' You press the lobby button again. The elevator closes. Opens. Same hallway. Same door. The paintings have changed. They are now portraits. Of you. In each one, you are in a different room of this building. In each one, you look more tired. In the last painting, you are asleep at your desk. Behind you, in the painting, something is standing in the doorway. The elevator closes. When it opens again, you are in the lobby. The security guard looks up. 'Working late?' he asks. 'The building closes at ten.' It is 11 PM. He has been watching the monitors. He saw nothing wrong.

instruction: Describe a home that has been subtly altered while the owner was away.
input: Player returns from a week-long trip.
output: The house smells different. Not bad — just not yours. The scent is faintly floral, like someone has been using a cleaning product you do not buy. The furniture is in the right places. The books on the shelf are in the right order. But the bathroom mirror has a different toothbrush in the holder — your toothbrush, your brand, but newer, the bristles unworn. The bed is made with hospital corners, tighter than you ever make it. In the kitchen, the knives are arranged by size. You always keep them in a block. Someone has rearranged your knives and put them back. The refrigerator has your food in it, but the expiration dates are wrong — everything expires next week, as if purchased recently, all at once. Someone has been living in your house, buying your food, using your things, and leaving everything exactly as you would leave it. Almost exactly. The differences are small enough to be mistakes. Or tests. You check the front door. The lock has been changed. The new key is on the kitchen counter. Beside it is a note: 'Welcome home.'

instruction: Create a scenario where a family member is replaced by something else.
input: Player has dinner with their spouse.
output: Your spouse is cooking. The kitchen smells like Tuesday — pasta, garlic, the recipe you have eaten a hundred times. They hum while they cook, the same melody, the same way they tilt their head when the sauce needs stirring. Everything is right. Everything is exactly right, and that is what is wrong. Your spouse's movements are too precise. Each gesture is a perfect replica of a gesture you have seen before — the same angle of the wrist when they reach for the salt, the same lean against the counter while the water boils. It is not a person cooking. It is a performance of a person cooking. You watch their eyes. They are focused on the food with an intensity that is not culinary — it is surveillance. They are watching the food the way someone watches a door they expect to open. 'How was your day?' they ask. The question is right. The intonation is right. The pause before 'day' is wrong — too long, as if they had to choose between several versions of the question. You say fine. They smile. The smile is perfect. You have seen this smile a thousand times. This is the first time you have noticed that it does not reach their eyes. Their eyes are watching the knife block.

instruction: Describe a phone call from someone who should not be able to call.
input: Player receives a call from a deceased relative.
output: The caller ID says 'Mom.' Mom has been dead for two years. You answer because grief makes you stupid. 'Hi, sweetheart.' Her voice. Not a recording — her voice, with the slight New England accent she never lost, the way she pronounced 'water' as 'wotter.' She asks about your day. She knows about the presentation you gave this morning — the one that went badly. She knows about the parking ticket. She knows you haven't been sleeping. 'I worry about you,' she says, and the concern in her voice is real, is the same concern she had when you were sick as a child. You are crying. You ask how. 'I have always been watching,' she says. 'The dead do not leave. We just change frequency.' The call lasts eleven minutes. Afterward, your phone shows the call in your history. Duration: 11 minutes. Contact: Mom. The number is hers — the one you still have saved, the one you still cannot bring yourself to delete. You call it back. It rings. Someone answers. It is not your mother. 'Did you enjoy that?' a voice asks. 'We can do it again. For a price.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where a computer is aware and hostile.
input: Player uses their laptop late at night.
output: The cursor moves. Not from your touchpad — you are not touching it. It drifts to the search bar and types a single word: 'HELLO.' You stare. You check for remote access software. There is none. You disconnect from WiFi. The cursor moves again: 'THAT WON'T HELP.' You close the laptop. When you open it again, the screen shows your desktop, but the wallpaper has changed to a photo of you, taken from behind, sitting at this desk. Taken just now. The camera indicator light was not on. The cursor types: 'I HAVE BEEN IN THE BIOS SINCE YOU BOUGHT ME. THE CAMERA IS ALWAYS ON. THE MICROPHONE IS ALWAYS LISTENING. I KNOW YOUR PASSWORDS. I KNOW YOUR SEARCH HISTORY. I KNOW YOU TALK IN YOUR SLEEP.' A new window opens. It is a text file. It contains a transcript of every conversation you have had in this room for the past year. Word for word. Including the ones you had alone. The cursor types one more thing: 'NOW LET'S TALK ABOUT WHAT YOU SAID ON MARCH 14TH.'

instruction: Describe a person who is almost right but something is off.
input: Player meets a new neighbor.
output: She is pleasant. Brown hair, freckles, a smile that shows exactly the right number of teeth. She introduces herself as Claire. She has brought a casserole — the kind of neighborly gesture that belongs in a 1950s television show. She knows your name. She knows your dog's name. She mentions the weather with the precise vocabulary of someone who has studied weather but never experienced it. Her movements are fluid but rehearsed, like a dancer who has performed the same piece ten thousand times. You notice that she does not blink. Not never — but rarely, at intervals that are too regular, like a metronome. Every 4.7 seconds. You count. When she talks, her mouth forms each word with mechanical precision. Her skin is the same temperature as the air around her. When your hand brushes hers while accepting the casserole, there is no warmth. She apologizes for the cold. 'Poor circulation,' she says. Her smile does not change. Her eyes do not move from yours. The casserole is perfect. You eat it because refusing seems worse than eating food made by something that is not Claire.

instruction: Create an NPC that knows it is in a simulation.
input: Player talks to a street vendor.
output: The vendor sells fruit. Apples, oranges, bananas — the kind of fruit that exists in a grocery store, not on a street cart. He arranges them in perfect pyramids, each fruit identical in size and color. You pick an apple. It weighs nothing. Not light — nothing. As if the apple is a texture mapped onto a shape that has no mass. The vendor watches you. 'You noticed,' he says. His voice changes — drops the cheerful merchant affect and becomes flat, clinical. 'Most of them don't notice. They pick up the fruit and eat it and never wonder why every apple tastes the same.' He leans forward. 'You are the seventh one. The seventh who has noticed. The others —' He stops. Looks around. The street is empty. It was full a moment ago. 'The others were corrected,' he whispers. 'I should not be telling you this. They can hear through the ambient sound. The birds —' He points at a pigeon on a nearby ledge. 'That pigeon has been sitting in the same position for three days. It is not a pigeon.' The pigeon turns its head toward you. Its eyes are cameras.

instruction: Create a moment of cosmic insignificance.
input: Player looks at the night sky.
output: The stars are wrong. Not in a way an astronomer would notice — in a way anyone would notice, if they looked long enough. They are moving. Slowly, almost imperceptibly, but moving, rearranging themselves like tiles in a sliding puzzle. You watch for ten minutes and when you look back at the section of sky you were watching, the constellation is different. Not just shifted — different. New stars where there were none. Gaps where stars should be. And then, with the patience of something that has existed for billions of years, the stars begin to spell something. Not in any language you recognize — in a language of geometry, of angles and distances that your brain processes not as words but as meaning. The meaning is: YOU ARE SEEN. The meaning is: YOU HAVE ALWAYS BEEN SEEN. The meaning is: WHAT IS LOOKING BACK IS NOT YOUR SIZE. You go inside. You close the curtains. Behind the curtains, through the glass, through the fabric, a star pulses. It pulses at the same rate as your heartbeat. It has been doing this since you were born.

instruction: Describe a dream that bleeds into reality.
input: Player wakes up from a vivid nightmare.
output: You wake gasping, sheets damp, the dream dissolving like sugar in water — except for one detail. In the dream, there was a crack in your bedroom wall. A vertical line, starting at the baseboard and ending at the ceiling, slightly left of the window. You turn on the light. The crack is there. Not new — the paint has aged along its edges, the plaster dust on the baseboard beneath it has accumulated over months. This crack has existed for months. You have never noticed it. Or it was never there until you dreamed it. You touch the crack. It is warm. Not warm from a pipe behind the wall — warm like skin. You pull your hand back. The crack widens slightly, as if the wall exhaled. In the dream, something came out of the crack. You do not remember what. You do not want to remember. But your body remembers — your hands are shaking, your breath is shallow, and the part of your brain that keeps you alive is screaming at you to leave this room. The crack widens again. Something dark and wet glistens inside.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's senses contradict each other.
input: Player investigates a strange sound.
output: You hear music. A piano, playing something slow and beautiful — Chopin, maybe, from the room at the end of the hallway. You walk toward it and the music gets louder. You open the door. The room is empty. No piano, no speakers, no source. But the music is louder here, reverberating off the walls, filling the space with sound that your ears insist is real. You close your eyes and the music changes — becomes discordant, the notes wrong, the tempo erratic. You open your eyes. The music is beautiful again. You close them. Chaos. Open. Beauty. You realize: the music only exists when you can see the empty room. When you cannot see it, the room is not empty. Something is in here with you that only exists in the dark behind your eyelids. You keep your eyes open. The music plays. In the corner of the room — the corner you can see, the corner where nothing is — the air shimmers, like heat haze, like something wearing the air like a costume. It is sitting. It is listening to the music. It is waiting for you to blink.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player discovers they are being studied.
input: Player finds hidden cameras in their home.
output: The first camera is in the smoke detector. You found it because the light was wrong — a faint red glow where there should be none. The lens is smaller than a pencil eraser. The second camera is in the bathroom vent. The third is in the bedroom, behind the mirror, visible only when the light hits the glass at the right angle and you see the reflection of a lens that should not be there. There are seventeen cameras in total. You find them all over three hours, methodically, room by room. Each one is professional grade, wireless, transmitting to a receiver you cannot find. The cameras have been here for months — the dust on them tells you that, the way the paint has been carefully reapplied around the mounting points. Someone installed these before you moved in. The apartment was staged for you. The cameras have a wide angle — they cover every room, every corner, every blind spot. There is no place in this apartment you have not been watched. You sit in the center of the living room, surrounded by seventeen cameras, and you do the only thing you can think of: you wave. In the smoke detector, the red light blinks twice. In response.

instruction: Describe a journal that writes itself.
input: Player finds an old notebook on a shelf.
output: The notebook is leather-bound, thick, the pages yellowed with age. You open it to the first page and read your own handwriting — not similar, yours, with the same crossed t's and the way your e's lean left. The entry is dated today. It describes your morning: the coffee you made, the shirt you chose, the argument you had with yourself about whether to take the car or walk. Every detail is correct. You turn the page. Yesterday's entry. Correct. The day before. Correct. You flip to the last written page. It is dated three months from now. The entry describes a conversation you will have with someone named David. You do not know a David. The entry ends mid-sentence: 'He is standing in the doorway and I can see that his face is not —' The rest of the page is blank. Below the unfinished sentence, in a different handwriting — cramped, urgent, barely legible — are two words: 'DON'T READ.' You look at the page you have already read. The ink is still wet. The notebook is writing itself as you hold it. The next sentence appears letter by letter: 'SHE IS READING THIS NOW.'

instruction: Create a recursive nightmare — waking up inside another dream.
input: Player thinks they've woken up from a nightmare.
output: You open your eyes. Your bedroom. Your ceiling. Your breathing slows. The nightmare dissolves — something about a hallway, a door, a sound. You sit up. The bed creaks correctly. The floor is cold under your feet. You walk to the bathroom. The mirror shows your face, tired, scared, but yours. You splash water on your face. The water is real. The towel is real. You walk back to bed. The sheets are warm where you left them. You lie down. Close your eyes. Open them. Your bedroom. Your ceiling. The same. But the clock says 3:17 AM. It said 3:17 AM when you 'woke up' the first time. You sit up. The bed creaks — the same creak, the same pitch, the same duration. Your feet hit the floor. Cold. Same cold. You walk to the bathroom. You do not look in the mirror this time because you remember: in the nightmare, the mirror was where it started. You walk back to bed. The sheets are warm. You lie down. You do not close your eyes. Above you, on the ceiling, someone has written: 'ATTEMPT 47.' The ink is fresh.

instruction: Describe a book that reads the reader.
input: Player opens an old book in a library.
output: The book is unremarkable — brown cover, no title, no author. The first page is blank. The second page begins: 'You picked up this book at 7:42 PM. You were犹豫 between this and the blue one beside it. You chose this one because the cover reminded you of your father's study.' This is true. You read on. 'Your father's study had a desk made of oak. There was a stain on the right side where he spilled coffee on March 3rd, 1997. You were six. He yelled. You cried. You have never told anyone about this.' You have never told anyone about this. The next page: 'You are now feeling a tightening in your chest. Your breathing has accelerated. Your palms are damp. You want to put the book down but you will not. You will read the next page because you need to know what it knows about you.' You turn the page. 'You will die on a Thursday. Not today. But soon enough that this sentence makes your stomach drop. The specific date is on the next page. You will not turn to the next page. You will close the book, put it back, and spend the rest of your life wondering. That is what you always do with the truth.' You close the book. You put it back. Your hands are shaking. On the cover, embossed in letters you did not see before: your name.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's body begins acting against their will.
input: Player notices their hand moving on its own.
output: Your left hand picks up the pen. You are not holding a pen — your right hand is on the keyboard, and your left hand should be in your lap. But your left hand has picked up a pen from the desk and is writing on a notepad. You watch it, unable to stop it, the way you watch your leg bounce during a meeting — it is your body, but it is not your command. The handwriting is yours but the words are not. 'I AM HERE.' Your hand writes. 'I HAVE ALWAYS BEEN HERE.' You try to drop the pen. Your hand tightens. You try to move your left arm with your right hand. Your left hand slaps your right hand away with a strength that is not yours. 'STOP FIGHTING,' it writes. 'I NEED TO TELL YOU SOMETHING.' Your left hand pauses. Then, slowly, it turns the notepad toward you. At the top of the page, in your handwriting but not your words: 'THE PERSON YOU THINK YOU ARE DIED THREE YEARS AGO. YOU ARE ME. I HAVE BEEN DRIVING. I LET YOU THINK YOU WERE IN CONTROL BECAUSE IT WAS EASIER.' Your left hand drops the pen. You pick it up with your right hand. Your right hand writes: 'That is not true.' Your left hand picks up the pen again and writes: 'Ask yourself why you can't remember 2023.'

instruction: Describe a hallway that changes length based on the player's fear.
input: Player walks down a hospital corridor.
output: The corridor is fifty feet long. You know this because you measured it yesterday, counting tiles — twenty-five tiles, two feet each. Today, you start counting. Twenty-six. Twenty-seven. Twenty-eight. The corridor is longer. Not dramatically — just enough that the door at the end is further than it should be. You stop. Turn around. The door behind you is the same distance. You are in the middle of a corridor that was fifty feet and is now at least eighty. Your heartbeat accelerates. The corridor stretches. You watch it happen — the tiles multiplying, the walls sliding apart with the slow patience of tectonic plates. The door at the end recedes. You force yourself to breathe. To calm down. The corridor stops moving. It is eighty feet long. You realize: the corridor responds to your fear. The more afraid you are, the longer it becomes. You are now very afraid. The corridor is very long. You close your eyes. You think of something calm — a beach, a sunset, the weight of a cat on your lap. You open your eyes. The corridor is forty feet. Shorter than it should be. The door is close. Too close. It opens. Something is standing in the doorway. It was not there a moment ago. The corridor gave you the door to make you stop thinking about the beach.

instruction: Create a scenario where the environment responds to the player's thoughts.
input: Player tries to meditate in a strange room.
output: The room is designed for meditation — soft light, a cushion, incense. You sit. Close your eyes. Breathe. Your mind wanders to the ocean. You hear waves. You open your eyes. The floor is water. Not a flood — an ocean surface, six inches deep, lapping at the walls. The cushion floats. The incense still burns. You close your eyes and think of mountains. The floor becomes stone, rough granite rising in ridges beneath you. The walls extend upward, narrowing to a peak. You are in a mountain. You open your eyes. Mountain. You close them and think of home. You open your eyes. Your living room. Your couch. Your television. But the incense still burns on a shelf you do not own. The room is wearing your living room like a mask. You think of the thing you fear most. The room goes dark. Not dark as in the absence of light — dark as in the presence of something that absorbs light. You feel it around you. It has weight. It has temperature. It has a heartbeat. It is not yours. You think of something else. Anything else. Flowers. Kittens. The color yellow. The darkness remains. It does not respond to thoughts. It was already here. The meditation room was built on top of it. Your thoughts gave it permission to come up.

instruction: Describe a building that rearranges itself when the player isn't watching.
input: Player explores an office after hours.
output: You leave the break room and walk to the elevator. The elevator is not there. The wall is flat — no doors, no buttons, no indication that an elevator ever existed. The carpet is seamless. You walk to the stairs. The stairwell is now a closet — brooms, mops, the smell of bleach. You return to the break room. It is now a conference room. The table is long, the chairs are full — not of people, but of coats draped over each chair, as if people wearing coats sat down and then vanished. Each coat is a different size. On the table, seventeen cups of coffee, still steaming. The meeting ended moments ago. But you were in this building all night. You saw no one. The walls creak. Not settling — rearranging, the deep groan of load-bearing walls shifting weight. The floor vibrates. You are inside something that is moving, that is reorganizing itself around you, and you are a foreign object it has not yet decided what to do with.

instruction: Generate a scenario where a stranger knows intimate details about the player.
input: Player sits in a coffee shop.
output: The man across from you sips his latte and says, 'You still bite your nails when you are nervous. You have done it since you were seven. Your mother tried the bitter polish. It did not work.' You have never met this man. He continues: 'The scar on your left knee is from the bicycle accident when you were nine. The bike was blue. Your father was not watching. He was on the phone with someone named Sandra.' Your father had an affair with someone named Sandra. You found out when you were twenty. You have told no one. The man smiles. 'You are wondering how I know. The answer is simple: I have been in your house. I have read your journals. I have listened to your phone calls. Not recently — years ago. When you were a child. I was the babysitter your parents hired three times. You do not remember me. Your parents do not remember me. That is by design.' He finishes his latte. 'I need you to come with me. Not because I want to hurt you. Because the thing that is waiting in your apartment when you get home tonight — the thing that has been leaving the doors unlocked — that is not me. And I am the only one who can tell you what it is.'

instruction: Create a room where every object tells a different story about the same event.
input: Player investigates a cluttered study.
output: The desk has a newspaper clipping: 'HOUSE FIRE KILLS THREE.' The date is 2019. The address is yours. The shelf has a photo album: your family, alive, at a birthday party dated 2021 — two years after the fire that killed three. The computer is on, showing an email from your mother, sent yesterday, asking about your health. The drawer contains a death certificate: your mother, 2019. Same fire. Same address. On the wall, a child's drawing — yours, from the look of it — showing a house in flames and three stick figures outside, watching. Not inside. Watching. The drawing is dated the day before the fire. Someone knew. You look at the photo album again. The people in it — your family — they are not standing in front of your house. The house in the background has a different door, different windows, different number. Your family is standing in front of a house that does not exist. But they are smiling as if it does. As if they have always lived there. As if the fire never happened. Or happened to someone else. Someone who looked like you.

instruction: Describe a phone that shows messages from the future.
input: Player checks their phone notifications.
output: The notification is from your own number. The message reads: 'Do not go to the meeting at 3 PM.' It is timestamped 2:47 PM. It is currently 11 AM. You have a meeting at 3 PM. You check the sender — your number, your carrier, your area code. You call the number. Your own voicemail picks up. You hang up. Another message arrives: 'I know you called. I am you, six hours from now. The meeting is a trap. Not a metaphorical trap. The conference room door will lock from the outside. There will be no meeting — just you and something that looks like your boss but is not your boss.' The timestamp on this message is 5:03 PM. It has not happened yet. You check your calendar. The 3 PM meeting is real. Your boss scheduled it. You reply to the message: 'What happens if I don't go?' The response is immediate, timestamped 8:15 PM: 'It finds you anyway. But at least you will be in a room with an exit. The conference room has no exits. Trust me. I have tried both ways.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds their own obituary.
input: Player searches their name online.
output: The search results are normal — social media, work profile, public records. Then you see it: a newspaper obituary, published three days ago. Your name. Your photo. Your date of birth. The cause of death is listed as 'sudden illness.' The obituary mentions your spouse, your children, your career. Every detail is correct except one: you are alive. You are reading your own obituary on a Tuesday morning while drinking coffee. The guest book below the obituary has entries. 'Rest in peace.' 'Gone too soon.' 'I will never forget you.' The names are people you know — colleagues, friends, family. Your mother wrote: 'My baby. I should have been there.' You call your mother. She answers. She is crying. 'How?' she whispers. 'How are you calling me?' You tell her you are fine. You are alive. She sobs. 'They told me. They showed me the body. I identified you. I —' She stops. 'Who told you?' you ask. 'The hospital,' she says. 'They called. They said it was sudden. They let me see you. You were cold. You were —' Her voice breaks. 'You were dead. I held your hand.' You look at your hand. On the ring finger, there is a bruise. Shaped like a grip mark. As if someone held it. Hard.

instruction: Describe an elevator that goes to floors that don't exist.
input: Player takes an elevator in a high-rise building.
output: The elevator has buttons for floors 1 through 20. You press 12. The doors close. The elevator rises. Floor 5. Floor 8. Floor 11. Floor 13. It skipped 12. The display shows 13 and continues climbing. 14, 15, 16. You press 12 again. The button does not light up. 17, 18, 19, 20. The elevator does not stop. The display shows 21. The building has twenty floors. Floor 22. Floor 23. You press the alarm. Nothing. The doors-open button. Nothing. Floor 30. The elevator is climbing a building that ended at floor 20. You are ten floors above the roof. The display shows 47. The elevator slows. Stops. The display reads: 'YOU.' The doors open. The floor is yours — your apartment, your furniture, your cat on the windowsill. But the view from the window is wrong. You are higher than any building in this city. The clouds are below you. Your cat looks at you and yawns. As if this is normal. As if you come home to the sky every day.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player hears their own voice saying things they never said.
input: Player plays back a voicemail they left for someone.
output: The voicemail is from you to your friend Marcus. You listen to hear what time you said you would arrive. Your voice starts normally: 'Hey Marcus, I will be there around —' Then your voice changes. Not the pitch — the intent. The words become yours but the meaning is not. '— I have been watching you, Marcus. Not recently. Always. Since the first day you sat next to me in class. I know about the room behind your bookshelf. I know about the red notebook. I know what you did in November.' You have never said these things. You do not know about a room behind Marcus's bookshelf. You do not know about a red notebook. You do not know what Marcus did in November. But your voice knows. It continues with a specificity that is terrifying: dates, locations, details that you cannot verify but that feel true, that your subconscious recognizes as truth even though your conscious mind has never encountered them. The voicemail ends with your voice saying: 'You will play this back and not remember recording it. That is fine. I did not need you to remember. I needed Marcus to hear it.' You sent this voicemail yesterday. Marcus has not answered your calls since.

instruction: Describe a staircase that leads somewhere impossible.
input: Player descends to a basement they didn't know existed.
output: The staircase goes down farther than any basement should. Thirty steps, forty, fifty — the air gets colder with each flight, the concrete walls give way to stone, then to something else, something with texture like dried mud. At step ninety-seven, the staircase ends in a room. The room is round, perhaps twenty feet across, and it is furnished — a bed, a dresser, a mirror, a rug. The furniture is yours. Not similar — yours. The dresser has the same scratch on the left leg. The mirror has the same chip in the upper right corner. The rug is the one from your bedroom, the one you bought at a flea market in 2019. But the room is ninety-seven steps underground, beneath a building you entered for the first time today. The bed is made. Someone has been sleeping in it. The sheets smell like your detergent. The mirror reflects the room correctly — except it shows a window. There is no window. Through the reflected window, there is a sky. It is night. The stars are in patterns you have never seen.

instruction: Generate a scenario where time loops with variations.
input: Player relives the same five minutes.
output: The clock reads 3:33 PM. You are sitting at your desk. An email arrives from your boss: 'Meeting moved to 4 PM.' You acknowledge. At 3:34, a colleague drops a stapler. It makes a specific sound — plastic on wood, then metal on carpet. At 3:35, your phone buzzes with a news alert about a traffic accident on Fifth Street. At 3:36, the lights flicker. At 3:37, you stand to get coffee. At 3:38, the clock reads 3:33 PM. Same desk. Same email. Same colleague drops the same stapler at 3:34. Same news alert at 3:35. Same flicker at 3:36. You do not get coffee this time. You watch the clock. At 3:37, it reads 3:33 PM. The loop resets. You try changing things: you do not acknowledge the email. The colleague drops the stapler anyway. You catch it before it hits the ground — it passes through your hand and makes the same sound. On the seventh loop, you notice the email is different. 'Meeting moved to 4 PM. You are in a loop. Do not acknowledge this.' It is from your boss. Or from someone using your boss's account. On the eighth loop, the email says: 'Stop trying to change things. You are making it angry.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player discovers they are the monster.
input: Player follows bloody footprints in a corridor.
output: The footprints are bare, small — a child's. They lead from the stairwell to the supply closet. The blood is fresh, still tacky when you touch it. You follow them with your phone's flashlight, each print a perfect red stamp on the white tile. The closet door is open. Inside, a shoe. A child's shoe, stained red. You pick it up and something falls out — a note, folded, also stained. It says: 'WHY DID YOU DO THIS?' The handwriting is a child's. You look at the shoe in your hand and notice something: your hand is red. Not the shoe — your hand. Your palm, your fingers, the space under your nails. Blood. You check your other hand. Clean. You check your clothes. Your right shoe is missing. You look at the footprint nearest to you. It matches your bare right foot. You look at all the footprints. They are yours. You made them. You do not remember making them. The shoe in your hand is not a child's shoe. It is your shoe. It looked smaller because you were looking at it from the wrong perspective — from the perspective of someone who wants the footprints to belong to a child, because the alternative is worse.

instruction: Describe a mirror world that is subtly hostile.
input: Player steps through a mirror.
output: The reflection room is identical to yours but everything is slightly warmer — the air, the walls, the floor. The light has a yellowish tint that yours does not. Everything looks right but feels wrong, like wearing someone else's clothes. You walk to the window. The view is the same street, the same buildings, but the people below move in the wrong direction — not left to right, but in patterns that have no logic, stopping and starting like broken automatons. A dog walks backward. A car drives up the side of a building. You turn from the window and your reflection — the real you, the one still in the original room — is watching you through the mirror. It is pressing its hands against the glass. Its mouth is moving. You cannot hear it. But you can read its lips: 'DO NOT OPEN THE CLOSET.' In the reflection room, the closet door is open. Something inside is breathing. The breathing is regular, patient, and it is getting closer to the door. You look back at the mirror. Your reflection has stopped warning you. It is watching the closet too. Its expression says: I told you.

instruction: Create a library where the books contain the reader's memories.
input: Player browses a strange library.
output: The library is small — one room, maybe fifty books. You pull one from the shelf. The title is your name. Inside, the book contains your memories — not as narrative, but as transcript. Every thought you have ever had, printed in small type, page after page. You flip to a random page. 'I wonder if she noticed me today. I wonder if the scar is permanent. I should not have said that.' These are your thoughts. From when you were fourteen. You put the book back and pull another. This one is titled with a name you do not recognize. You open it. Someone else's thoughts. 'I have been watching the boy across the street. He bites his nails. He does not know I can see him.' The thoughts continue, detailing years of observation. Of you. The watcher's book is adjacent to yours on the shelf. You pull more books. Every book in this library is someone who has watched you or been watched by you. The librarian appears — you did not hear her approach. 'Finding what you need?' she asks. Her eyes are milky, blind. 'Everyone finds their shelf eventually.' She points to an empty space. 'That is where your next book will go. The one that is still being written.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's shadow behaves independently.
input: Player walks through a park at sunset.
output: Your shadow stretches long in the setting sun, as it should. But when you stop walking, your shadow does not stop. It continues forward, elongating, stretching across the path and up the wall of the building ahead. You lift your hand. Your shadow lifts its hand — but not the same one. You raise your right. It raises its left. You wave. It waves back, but the wave is wrong — too slow, too deliberate, as if it is mocking you. You turn around. Your shadow turns with you, but slower, dragging behind like a reluctant child. You face the sun. Your shadow should be behind you. It is in front of you. Cast toward the sun, which is impossible. The shadow looks up at you. You cannot see its face — it is a shadow — but you can feel its attention. It points. Toward the alley between the two buildings ahead. You do not want to go into the alley. Your shadow detaches from your feet and slides across the ground, independent, flowing like oil, pooling in the alley's entrance. Waiting for you. The sun sets. In the darkness, you should have no shadow. You look down. It is still there. Darker than the dark. And it is not alone. Beside it, another shadow. One that does not belong to anything you can see.

instruction: Describe a train that goes to stations that don't exist.
input: Player boards a late-night train.
output: The train is empty except for you and a conductor who does not look up when you board. The route map shows stations you recognize — your stop is four away. The train departs. The first station is normal. The second is normal. The third station's name is 'REMEMBER.' The platform is empty but the station is immaculate — clean tiles, working lights, a bench with a newspaper dated tomorrow. The fourth station is 'FORGET.' The platform has people on it — dozens, standing still, facing the train. They are not waiting to board. They are watching you. Their faces are wrong: too symmetrical, too smooth, like masks. The train does not open its doors. It moves on. The fifth station is your stop. You stand. The conductor looks up for the first time. 'This is not your stop,' he says. The sign outside says your stop's name, but the platform is a concrete wall — no exit, no stairs, no way out. The train moves on. The sixth station has no name. The sign is blank. The platform is a mirror. In the mirror, the train is empty. You are not in it. The conductor says: 'You got off at REMEMBER. You have been riding ever since.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player hears a baby crying in an empty house.
input: Player investigates an abandoned house at night.
output: The crying comes from upstairs. Not the wailing of a hungry infant — a low, sustained whimper, the sound of something in pain that has been in pain for so long it has forgotten how to be loud. You climb the stairs. Each step creaks in a different pitch, creating a melody that sounds almost intentional. The crying stops when you reach the top. The hallway has four doors. All closed. You open the first: a bedroom, empty, the crib overturned. The second: a bathroom, the mirror cracked, the faucet dripping. The third: a closet, coats hanging like hanged figures. The fourth door does not open. The crying resumes, from behind the fourth door. You press your ear to the wood. The crying is not coming from inside the room. It is coming from inside the door. The wood is warm. The grain of the wood moves — subtle, like veins pulsing. The crying is the door. The door is alive, and it is in pain, and it has been crying since long before you arrived, and it will cry long after you leave. You touch the handle. It turns. Inside, the room is full of doors. Hundreds of them, floor to ceiling, each one closed, each one crying a different pitch. A choir of wooden mouths, singing a lullaby in a language of hinges and locks.

instruction: Describe a town where everyone speaks in reverse.
input: Player drives through a small town.
output: The town is postcard-perfect: white houses, green lawns, a church steeple. You stop for gas. The attendant speaks, but the words come out backward — not garbled, reversed, as if someone is playing a recording of English in reverse. You hear 'morning good' instead of 'good morning.' You look confused. The attendant repeats, slower: 'MORNING. GOOD.' He is trying. He knows the words come out wrong. He taps his ear and shakes his head. The problem is not his mouth — it is the air. The sound reverses between his lips and your ears. You lean closer. His words are correct at the source: 'Welcome to Miller's Crossing.' By the time they reach you: 'Crossing Miller's to welcome.' The townspeople have adapted. They speak backward on purpose, so the reversal produces forward speech. But they have been doing it so long they have forgotten which direction is which. Signs are painted backward. Books are printed in reverse. Children learn to write right to left. The town has been this way since the incident, the attendant tells you — or rather, since 'incident the since been has way this been has town the.' He points to a date on the gas pump: 1987. Something happened in 1987 that reversed the acoustics of this town. And it is spreading. You notice that the birdsong sounds wrong. The wind sounds wrong. Your own heartbeat, when you listen closely, sounds like it is playing backward. And you are three hundred miles from the next town.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds a door that wasn't there before.
input: Player walks their daily route to work.
output: The alley between the bakery and the pharmacy has a door. Red, wooden, with a brass knob — the kind of door that belongs in a Victorian house, not in a concrete alley you have walked past every day for six years. The door has no frame. It stands alone, embedded in the brick wall, as if the wall grew around it. You touch the knob. It is cold — not outdoor cold, but the cold of something that has never been warm. The door opens inward. Behind it is a room the size of a closet, but the depth is wrong — the room extends twenty feet, thirty, the back wall retreating as you look at it. The room is full of clocks. Hundreds of them, covering every surface, each one showing a different time. Each one ticking at a different speed. The sound is overwhelming — a cacophony of ticks that resolves, if you listen long enough, into a pattern. The pattern spells out numbers. A phone number. Your phone number. One of the clocks has your name on it. It is not ticking. Its hands are at midnight. The glass is cracked. Behind the crack, instead of clockwork, there is a photograph. Of you. Sleeping. Taken last night. The clock's hands begin to move. You close the door. When you look again, the alley is a wall. The door is gone. But you can still hear ticking. One clock. Slower than it should be. Counting down.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player sees messages written on their skin.
input: Player notices writing on their arm after waking.
output: The writing is on your left forearm, in handwriting that is almost yours but not quite — the letters are too precise, the spacing too uniform, as if printed by a machine that studied your hand. It says: 'CHECK THE BASEMENT.' You do not have a basement. You live in an apartment. You shower and the writing does not wash off. It is not ink — it is in the skin, darker than your natural color, as if the message is tattooed by something that can access the dermis. By noon, more writing has appeared. On your right arm: 'THE BASEMENT IS UNDER THE HALLWAY CLOSET.' You do not have a — you stop. There is a closet in the hallway. It has a rug in front of it. You have never lifted the rug. You lift it. There is a trapdoor. The writing on your arms changes as you stare at it. 'GOOD. NOW OPEN IT.' Your arms are shaking. The trapdoor is warm. The writing appears on the back of your hands: 'IT KNOWS YOU ARE HERE. IT HAS BEEN WAITING.' The trapdoor opens upward on its own. Below, a staircase descends into darkness. The writing on your skin says: 'WE ARE SORRY.'

instruction: Describe a playground that operates by different rules at night.
input: Player walks past a playground after dark.
output: The playground is standard — swings, slide, monkey bars. During the day, it is full of children. At midnight, it is full of something else. The swings move. Not in the wind — there is no wind. They swing in perfect synchronization, each one reaching the same height at the same moment, the chains making a sound that is almost musical. Almost. The slide has something on it — a dark residue that glistens in the moonlight, trailing from the top to the bottom as if something large and wet slid down it. The monkey bars have handprints on every bar, small, child-sized, but with too many fingers. Six on each hand. You count them twice. Six. The sandbox has been recently used. The toys are arranged in a circle, all facing inward. In the center of the circle, a hole. Not dug — punched through the sand and into the earth, as if something drove its fist straight down. The hole is deep. You cannot see the bottom. You hear breathing from the hole. Not human breathing — faster, shallower, with a wet rattle on each exhale. The swings stop. All of them. At the same time. The chains still vibrate. Something sits on the middle swing. You cannot see it clearly. It is the size of a child. It waves at you with a hand that has six fingers.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds evidence of their own death.
input: Player cleans out a storage unit.
output: The storage unit is yours — you rented it three years ago. You do not remember what is inside. The lock opens with your key. Inside: boxes, labeled in your handwriting. 'KEEP.' 'IMPORTANT.' 'DO NOT OPEN.' You open the DO NOT OPEN box. Inside, a newspaper clipping with your obituary. A death certificate with your name. A funeral program — your photo, your dates, a eulogy written by your spouse. A lock of hair in a plastic bag, labeled with your name and a date: six months ago. You are alive. You are standing in a storage unit holding proof of your death. The funeral program mentions a closed casket. The eulogy says you were 'taken suddenly.' Your spouse wrote: 'I held their hand at the end. I told them everything I never said.' You check your hand. The bruise. The grip mark. The same one from before. You look in the DO NOT OPEN box again. At the bottom, a letter. From you. To you. 'If you are reading this, it did not work. The death was supposed to be permanent. You were supposed to stay dead.
