The thing that woke up in the hospital is not the thing that died. I know because I am the one who died. And I am writing this from the other side. You need to stop looking for answers. You need to stop existing. Because if you keep existing, it means one of us is wrong. And I have proof that it is you.'

instruction: Create a library book that predicts the reader's death.
input: Player borrows a book from a library.
output: The book is fiction — a thriller, the kind with a dark cover and a one-word title. You read the first chapter on the bus home. The protagonist is a man named David who lives in a city like yours, works in an office like yours, has a dog like yours. In chapter two, David finds a book at the library. The book-within-a-book describes a man named David who finds a book at the library. You stop reading. The recursion is deliberate — the author is playing with metafiction, you tell yourself. You continue. In chapter three, David's dog goes missing. You get home. Your dog is gone. Not escaped — gone. The leash is still by the door. The collar is on the floor. The dog is not in the apartment. You check the book. Chapter four: David searches for his dog and finds a note that says 'CHECK THE FIRE ESCAPE.' You check your fire escape. There is a note. It says 'CHECK CHAPTER FIVE.' Chapter five describes David's death. You do not read chapter five. You close the book. On the back cover, there is a photo of the author. It is you. The publication date is 2031. The book was written by you, five years from now, and it describes how you die. You put the book in a drawer and lock it. Tomorrow you will return it. Tonight you will not sleep. In the morning, the book is on your nightstand. Open to chapter five. You did not open it. The bookmark is on page 247. The last page. The last sentence reads: 'And David closed the book, knowing that he had already read the ending, and that the ending was him.'

instruction: Describe a restaurant where the food is memories.
input: Player eats at an unfamiliar restaurant.
output: The menu has no prices and no dish names — just years. '2003.' '2015.' '1998.' You order 2003. The waiter — who has no face, just smooth skin where features should be — brings a plate. On it, a single bite of food. You eat it. The taste is not food — it is a summer afternoon, the smell of cut grass, the sound of a screen door slamming, the feeling of being eight years old and invincible. The memory is yours. You have not thought about this day in twenty years. Tears run down your face. The waiter returns. 'Dessert?' he asks. You point at 2019. The dessert is bitter — the taste of a hospital waiting room, the fluorescent hum, the phone call that changed everything. You taste your mother's death. Not the event — the emotion. The specific cocktail of grief and relief and guilt. You push the plate away. The waiter nods. 'Most cannot finish 2019,' he says. His voice is warm. Almost kind. 'The kitchen has one more course. On the house.' He places a small dish in front of you. The year on the dish is next year. You do not eat it. You do not want to taste the future. But the dish moves closer on its own, and the smell — the smell is already in your nose, already triggering a memory that has not happened yet. The memory is of you, in this restaurant, refusing to eat, and then eating. You are already remembering the decision you have not yet made.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player discovers a hidden room in their home that contains evidence they are not who they think they are.
input: Player finds a room behind their basement wall.
output: The wall sounds hollow. You knock again. Definitely hollow. Behind a shelf of paint cans, a section of drywall is newer than the rest. You pull it away. Behind it: a room. Small, maybe six by six feet, with a desk, a lamp, a filing cabinet. The desk has a nameplate with a name that is not yours. The filing cabinet contains files — birth certificates, passports, photos. All of one person. All with different names. All with your face. The earliest document is dated before you were born. The photos show you in places you have never been, wearing clothes you have never owned, with people you have never met. But you are smiling in every photo. A genuine smile — the kind that crinkles the eyes. In the bottom drawer, a journal. The last entry is dated the day you moved into this house. It reads: 'New identity. New city. The transfer was clean. They will not find me here. I have buried the old ones deep enough.' The handwriting is yours. You do not remember writing it. You do not remember being anyone else. But the evidence is extensive, meticulous, and personal. Someone with your face has been living many lives. And they were careful enough to forget each one. Including the current one.

instruction: Describe a radio station that only broadcasts at 3 AM.
input: Player tunes through radio frequencies late at night.
output: Between the static and the Spanish station, at 91.7 FM, a voice. Not music — a voice, reading names. Your name is the third one. The voice says your full name, your date of birth, your address, and then: 'Status: listening.' You turn off the radio. The voice continues from the speaker. The radio is off but the speaker is on. The voice reads another name. Then another. It is reading a list. Each name is followed by a status: 'sleeping,' 'driving,' 'crying,' 'dying.' The last status is 'dying.' The voice pauses. Then: 'New entry. [Your name]. Status: afraid.' It knows. It is not recording you — it is narrating you, in real time, to whoever is listening on 91.7 FM at 3 AM. You unplug the radio. The voice continues from the wall. From the outlet. From the wiring in the house. The voice is in the electrical system. It reads another name. Someone you know. 'Status: in danger.' The voice reads your name again. 'Status: warning others.' You pick up the radio and throw it against the wall. The voice says: 'Status: violent. Updating file.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds a room that shows alternate versions of their life.
input: Player opens a door that shouldn't lead anywhere.
output: The door is in the basement, behind the water heater. It should open to a concrete wall. It opens to a room with nine windows. Each window shows a different scene — not a view, but a scene, like a television screen showing a life. In window one, you are a doctor, in a white coat, in a hospital you have never seen. In window two, you are in prison, older, thinner, staring at a wall. In window three, you are dead — a funeral, your photo, your spouse crying. In window four, you are a child, but the year is wrong — the calendar on the wall says 1923. In window five, you are not human — you are something else, something with too many limbs, moving through a space that has too many dimensions. In window six, you are sitting in this room, looking at these windows, in a room that has ten windows instead of nine. The tenth window shows you looking at a room with eleven windows. The recursion is infinite. In window seven, you are reading a description of this room. Right now. In window eight, you are writing the description. In window nine, the window is dark. Not empty — dark. Something is pressed against the glass from the other side. It breathes. The glass fogs. In the fog, a finger writes: 'CHOOSE ONE.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player's dreams are being harvested.
input: Player wakes up with a device attached to their head.
output: The device is small — metallic, warm, adhered to your temple with a substance that is not adhesive but has grip, like a magnet on steel. You pull it off. It comes away with a faint pop and a tingle that is not painful but is not pleasant. The device has a screen. On the screen, a list of dates. Each date has a title: 'The Ocean Dream.' 'The Falling Dream.' 'The One With Your Father.' These are your dreams. Your recurring dreams, cataloged with dates and durations. The device has been recording your dreams. The latest entry is last night — 'The Teeth Dream,' duration 47 minutes, REM quality: 94%. Below the title, a note: 'Purchased by user_7742. Payment: $0.03.' Your dreams are being sold. For three cents each. The device has a logo on the back — a company you have never heard of. Below the logo: 'SweetDreams Inc. Making your unconscious profitable since 2024.' The device has been on your head for at least a year. The list of recorded dreams goes back fourteen months. You have been sleeping with a machine that harvests your dreams and sells them to strangers. And you have never noticed. Because the device also ensures you do not notice. It says so in the manual, which is taped to the back: 'Do not remove during active use. May cause awareness of removal. If awareness occurs, administer reset protocol.' You hear a knock at your door. The reset protocol is here.

instruction: Describe a staircase where each step shows a different memory.
input: Player climbs a spiral staircase in an old building.
output: The first step shows your fifth birthday — not a photo, but the memory itself, playing in the air like a hologram, complete with the smell of cake and the sound of paper tearing. The second step: your first day of school, the weight of the backpack, the anxiety in your stomach. Third step: a kiss, a first kiss, the specific softness and the way time stopped. Each step is a memory, playing in order, chronological, climbing with you. By the twentieth step, the memories reach adulthood. The thirtieth step: a memory you have been trying to forget. It plays anyway. The forty-fifth step: a memory you do not recognize. You are in a room you have never seen, talking to a person you have never met, doing something you have never done. But the memory is yours — it has the same texture, the same emotional weight as all the others. The fifty-second step: a memory of the future. You are older. You are in a hospital bed. Someone is holding your hand. The memory is vivid, detailed, and inevitable. The sixty-first step: the staircase ends. But there are more memories. They play in the air above you, above the staircase, receding into darkness. The last visible memory is of you, standing on this staircase, looking up at memories you have not yet made. And in the memory, there is someone standing behind you on the stairs. Watching you look. They are not in any of your other memories. They are only in this one. The one that has not happened yet.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player discovers their entire life is a cover story.
input: Player finds their birth certificate has been altered.
output: The birth certificate is yours — correct hospital, correct date, correct parents. But the paper is wrong. Under ultraviolet light — you check with a blacklight from the bathroom — the certificate reveals a second layer. A different name. A different date. A different set of parents. The original certificate was erased and yours was printed on top. The erasure was professional — you only found it because the UV light revealed the chemical signature of the bleaching agent. The original name is illegible, but the original birthdate is thirty years before yours. The original parents' names are partially visible: the mother's first name is 'Helena.' You have never known a Helena. The hospital listed on the original certificate does not exist. You search for it online. It was demolished in 1995. The site is now a parking lot. You drive to the parking lot. Under the concrete, if you could dig, there would be a basement. In the basement, there would be records. In the records, there would be a person who was born thirty years before you, to a woman named Helena, in a hospital that was demolished, whose birth certificate was bleached and replaced with yours. And that person would look exactly like you. Because that person is you. Or you are them. The distinction is the part that scares you.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player hears music that no one else can hear.
input: Player hears a melody in a crowded place.
output: The melody is coming from everywhere and nowhere — a piano, playing something slow and beautiful, in a key that is almost familiar but not quite. You look around. No one reacts. The crowd flows around you, oblivious, while the music fills the space between them. You tap a stranger's shoulder. 'Do you hear that?' They look at you like you are insane. 'Hear what?' The music gets louder. Not louder — closer. As if the piano is approaching. You follow the sound. It leads you out of the crowd, down a side street, into an alley. The music is coming from a window on the second floor. The window is dark. The music stops. A face appears in the window. Your face. Looking down at you. The face smiles. The music resumes — but now it is coming from inside your head, not from the window. The melody has words now, singing in a voice that is yours but better — richer, more confident, more alive. The words are: 'You left the oven on. You left the door unlocked. You left the window open. You left me inside. You left me inside. You left me inside.' You run home. The oven is off. The door is locked. The window is open. You always close the window. On the windowsill, a single piano key. Ivory. Warm.

instruction: Describe an ally whose behavior becomes increasingly suspicious.
input: Player's trusted companion does something unexplained.
output: Marcus volunteers for the night watch. He always volunteers for the night watch. You have traveled together for two weeks and every night he offers — not asks, offers — to stay awake while you sleep. You accept because you are tired and he seems reliable. Tonight, you pretend to sleep. At 2:17 AM, Marcus stands. He walks to the supply bag and removes something small — you cannot see what. He walks to the door, opens it silently, and steps outside. He is gone for eleven minutes. When he returns, he puts something back in the bag. In the morning, you check the bag. Nothing seems missing. But one of the water bottles is empty — it was full last night. And the ground outside the door has footprints: Marcus's boots, leading to a tree fifty feet away, and then — the footprints stop. They do not return. Marcus walked to the tree and then his footprints vanish. But he came back. He is sleeping five feet away from you. The only footprints that lead back to the door are yours — from when you checked outside this morning. Marcus walked to that tree and came back without touching the ground.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player suspects they are being poisoned.
input: Player notices their food tastes slightly off.
output: The water tastes metallic. Not strongly — a faint copper note, the kind you would ignore if you were not paying attention. You are paying attention because yesterday the coffee tasted the same, and the day before the soup had a chemical undertone that you attributed to the seasoning. Three days of metallic taste. Three days of fatigue that sleep does not cure. Three days of headaches that start behind your left eye and spread. You test the water: you pour a glass and leave it on the counter. You fill a second glass from the same tap and leave it beside the first. You go to work. When you return, the first glass is empty. The second glass is full. You live alone. You did not drink the first glass. Someone was in your apartment and drank your water and left the second glass untouched. Or: the first glass evaporated. Or: something in your apartment drank it. You taste the second glass. Metallic. You taste the tap water directly. Normal. The contamination is in the glass, not the water. Someone is treating your glasses with something. Every glass you own. Every cup, every mug. You have been drinking it for three days. The symptoms match a slow-acting sedative. Someone wants you drowsy. Someone wants you compliant. Someone has a key to your apartment and visits while you are at work.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player finds surveillance equipment they didn't install.
input: Player discovers a microphone in their smoke detector.
output: The smoke detector chirps. Low battery. You twist it off the ceiling and find, taped to the inside, a device that is not part of the detector. It is a microphone — small, professional, with a battery that is definitely not low. The chirping came from the device, not the detector. A signal. You pull it out. It is warm. Not from the ceiling — from use. Active use. It was transmitting when you took it down. You look at the device. It has a brand you do not recognize and a frequency printed on the back: 433.92 MHz. You search the frequency. It is a common surveillance frequency. The range is three hundred feet. Someone is within three hundred feet of your apartment, listening. You search the rest of the apartment. You find six more: in the bathroom vent, behind the kitchen clock, inside the couch cushion, under the desk, in the bedroom lamp, and — this one makes you stop — sewn into the lining of the jacket you wear every day. Seven microphones. Covering every room. The jacket one means they can hear you outside too. You sit in the center of your living room, surrounded by seven microphones you have removed, and you say out loud: 'I know you are listening.' Nothing happens. Then your phone buzzes. A text from an unknown number: 'We know.'

instruction: Describe a situation where the player's memories don't match recorded evidence.
input: Player watches security footage from their workplace.
output: The footage is from last Tuesday, 3:15 PM. You are at your desk — you remember being at your desk. But on the screen, you are not at your desk. You are in the conference room. You are not alone. You are talking to someone — a person in a dark coat whose face is turned away from the camera. The conversation lasts forty-seven minutes. You do not remember any of it. You do not remember leaving your desk. You do not remember the conference room. You do not remember the person in the coat. The footage shows you laughing at one point. Laughing. With a person you have never met, in a room you were never in, for forty-seven minutes that do not exist in your memory. After the conversation, you return to your desk. On the footage, you sit down and immediately begin typing. You check your email for that day. At 4:02 PM — right after the meeting — you sent an email to yourself. The subject line is: 'DO NOT WATCH THE FOOTAGE.' The body is empty. You sent this email knowing that you would eventually watch the footage. You sent it forty-seven minutes ago. Or you sent it last Tuesday. The timeline is confusing because your memory is not a reliable narrator. The footage is. And the footage says you are not who you think you are on Tuesdays at 3:15 PM.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player realizes someone has been entering their home.
input: Player notices small changes in their apartment each day.
output: The bathroom towel is on the left. You always hang it on the right. You fix it. The next morning, it is on the left again. The book on your nightstand — you were on page 147. It is now on page 203. You live alone. You check the locks: intact. The windows: sealed. The fire escape: no sign of entry. But the changes continue. A glass moves from the cabinet to the counter. A chair rotates three degrees. The shower curtain, which you leave open, is closed. These are not the changes of a burglar — nothing is missing. These are the changes of someone who lives in your space when you are not here. Someone who uses your things, reads your books, dries their hands on your towel. Someone who is careful but not careful enough. Or someone who wants you to notice. You set a trap: you photograph every room before you leave. When you return, you compare. The photos show the changes — the moved glass, the rotated chair. But they also show something else. In the last photo, taken in the bedroom, there is a shape in the corner that was not there when you took the photo. A dark shape, roughly human, standing in the corner behind the door. You took this photo at 8 AM. The shape was there at 8 AM. In your bedroom. While you were taking the photo. You did not see it with your eyes. The camera did.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player suspects their therapist is manipulating them.
input: Player notices patterns in their therapy sessions.
output: Dr. Reeves always asks about your father. Every session. You have been coming for six months and every session, regardless of what you want to discuss, the conversation circles back to your father. The questions are specific: 'What did your father keep in the basement?' 'Did your father ever take you to the cabin?' 'Tell me about the red box.' You have never mentioned a cabin. You have never mentioned a red box. Your father did not have a basement. But the questions feel familiar — not because you have answered them before, but because they trigger something. Images. Not memories — images that feel implanted, like photographs slipped into a photo album. A cabin in the woods. A red box on a shelf. Your father's hands, opening the box. These are not your memories. But they are in your head, and Dr. Reeves put them there. You realize this during session twenty-three. Dr. Reeves is not helping you remember. Dr. Reeves is helping you forget. The real memories — whatever you are actually suppressing — are being overwritten with a fiction. A fiction about a cabin, a red box, a father who did things that never happened. Why? You check Dr. Reeves's credentials. They are impeccable. You check his patient list. You are his only patient. His entire practice exists for you.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's digital footprint shows activities they never did.
input: Player Googles their own name.
output: The search results include a blog. Your name, your photo, your writing style. The blog has been active for three years. Hundreds of posts about topics you care about — the same opinions, the same tone, the same way you structure an argument. But you have never written a blog. The posts reference events you attended — conferences, meetups, protests. Photos show you at these events. You were not at these events. But the photos are definitely you — same clothes you own, same haircut you had that month, same way you hold a coffee cup. Someone is living your life in the gaps — in the hours you do not account for, in the weekends you spent alone, in the evenings you do not remember clearly. The blog has comments. People who know you. 'Great seeing you Saturday!' 'Loved your talk.' 'Let's do lunch.' These people have met the other you. The you that goes to events and writes blogs and has a social life you know nothing about. The blog's most recent post is from yesterday. It describes a dinner party you hosted. In your apartment. With people you have never met. The post includes a photo of your living room, rearranged for the party. When you get home, your living room is rearranged. Exactly like the photo.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds a room in their house they've never seen.
input: Player opens a door they assumed was a closet.
output: The door has been a closet for the three years you have lived here. Brooms, coats, the vacuum. You open it today and the closet is gone. In its place: a room. Large — too large, the size of the room should not fit in the wall where the closet was. The room has a desk, a computer, a chair. The computer is on. The screen shows a live feed of your apartment — every room, from angles that match the microphones you found. Someone has been watching you from inside your own wall. The desk has papers. Maps of your daily route. A schedule of your movements. A list of your contacts with notes: 'reliable,' 'distracted,' 'potential threat.' Your name is at the top of a whiteboard. Below your name: 'Subject has begun noticing. Phase 2 recommended.' The chair is warm. Someone was sitting in it moments ago. The computer's keyboard is warm. There is a half-finished cup of coffee on the desk. The coffee is your brand. Made in your machine. The person in this room has been making coffee in your kitchen while you sleep. The room has no other exit. Whoever was here is still in your apartment. They are either behind you or they left through the closet door while you were looking at the computer. You check behind you. Nothing. You check the apartment. Empty. But the front door is unlocked. You locked it this morning.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player discovers they are being followed by someone who looks exactly like them.
input: Player sees their doppelganger in a crowd.
output: You see yourself across the street. Not someone who looks like you — you. Same face, same jacket, same scar above the left eyebrow. You freeze. Your double is standing at a bus stop, looking at a phone — your phone model, your case. The double looks up. Makes eye contact. Does not react. As if seeing yourself across the street is expected. The double boards a bus. You follow in your car. The bus stops at your workplace. The double gets off, badges in — you see the badge flash, the same lanyard. The double walks into your building. You park and follow. The double is at your desk. Your coworkers do not react. They greet the double by name. Your name. The double responds in your voice. You watch from the hallway as the double does your job — answers your emails, attends your meetings, eats your lunch. At 5 PM, the double leaves. You follow. The double walks to your apartment. Uses your key. Goes inside. You stand outside your own apartment, locked out, watching through the window as the double sits on your couch, turns on your television, and lives your life. The double looks at the window. At you. It smiles. Not a threatening smile — a tired one. The smile of someone who has been doing this for a long time. The smile says: 'Your turn is over.'

instruction: Describe a situation where all the player's contacts receive messages they didn't send.
input: Player's friends accuse them of sending disturbing messages.
output: Your sister calls, crying. 'Why would you send me that?' She forwards the message. It is from your number: a photo of her house, taken from outside, at night. The timestamp is 3 AM. You were asleep. Your friend Marcus sends a screenshot: a message from you saying 'I know what you did.' Marcus asks what you know. You know nothing. Your boss forwards an email from you, sent at 2:15 AM: a resignation letter, eloquent, detailed, referencing specific workplace incidents you do not remember. HR has already processed it. Your phone shows these messages in your outbox. You did not send them. But they came from your device, your number, your accounts. You change your passwords. The messages continue. You turn off your phone. Your friends receive messages from your email. You disconnect from the internet. Your neighbors receive handwritten notes in your mailbox. In your handwriting. You destroy your phone, your laptop, your router. Your sister receives a letter, postmarked from your zip code, written on your stationery, in your hand: 'I am sorry about the photos. I will stop. For now.' You have no stationery. You have never owned stationery. But the letter is on paper you recognize — paper you have seen before, in a desk you do not own, in a room you have never visited. The handwriting expert your sister hires confirms: it is your handwriting. All of it. Every message. Yours.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player discovers someone has been impersonating them for years.
input: Player meets people who know 'them' but whom they've never met.
output: A woman at a coffee shop hugs you. 'It has been so long!' She knows your name, your job, your dog's name. She references a trip to Portland you never took. A man at the gym waves. 'How is the novel coming?' You are not writing a novel. A child in the park runs to you. 'Uncle [your name]!' You have no nieces or nephews. Everyone in this neighborhood knows you — not the real you, but a version of you that has lived here for years, that goes to coffee shops and gyms and parks, that is writing a novel and has a niece and went to Portland. This version has your face, your voice, your mannerisms. It is so convincing that when you deny knowing these people, they look hurt. Confused. 'You were just here last week,' the woman says. 'You ordered the same thing you always order.' She looks at the barista. The barista nods. 'Oat milk latte, extra shot, no foam.' It is your order. You have never been to this coffee shop. But someone who is you has been coming here for years. And they ordered your drink. They knew your order before you did. Because they have been practicing you. Learning you. Becoming you. And the question that keeps you awake is: what happens when they are better at being you than you are?

instruction: Create a scenario where the player finds a camera hidden in a gift they received.
input: Player opens a birthday present from a friend.
output: The gift is from Sarah — a book, wrapped in paper you recognize from her apartment. You open it. Inside the cover, a small lens. Embedded in the cardboard, invisible unless you know where to look. A pinhole camera, wireless, with a battery that has been running for at least a week — the timestamp on the photos it has taken starts seven days ago. Seven days of you in your apartment. Reading. Sleeping. Changing. The camera has been in the book on your shelf for seven days, watching. Sarah gave you this book at dinner last Tuesday. She wrapped it herself. She hugged you when you opened it. She watched you put it on the shelf. She knew. You check Sarah's social media. Her photos are normal — friends, food, sunsets. But in the background of a photo from last month, you see yourself. Walking down a street you do not remember walking down. Sarah was following you. Taking photos of you from a distance. You confront Sarah. She does not deny it. 'I was worried about you,' she says. 'You have been acting strange.' You ask what she means. 'You have been going places you do not remember. Doing things you do not remember. I wanted to understand.' She shows you the photos. Hundreds. Over months. You in places you have never been. Doing things you have never done. Smiling in ways you do not smile. Sarah was not stalking you. She was documenting the other you. The one that comes out when you are not paying attention.

instruction: Describe a situation where the player's social media accounts post content they didn't create.
input: Player's Instagram posts a photo they never took.
output: The photo is of you, in a place you have never been — a rooftop, at night, a city skyline you do not recognize. You are smiling. The caption reads: 'Finally here.' The photo has fifty likes. Comments: 'So jealous!' 'When did you go?' 'You look great.' You do not remember taking this photo. You do not own the jacket you are wearing. You have never seen this skyline. EXIF data shows the photo was taken with your phone, at 11:47 PM last Friday. Last Friday you were home. You check your phone's location history. At 11:47 PM last Friday, your phone was on that rooftop. You were not with it. Or you were and do not remember. Your phone's photo gallery has more photos from that night. You at a bar. You at a restaurant. You with people you do not know. In each photo, you are happy — genuinely happy, in a way your photos never show. Someone is using your body to live a better life than you. And they are documenting it on your social media, as if daring you to notice. A new post appears. Live. Right now. You are in the photo. You are wearing the jacket. You are on the rooftop. You are smiling at the camera. You are not in this room. You are on the rooftop. You do not remember going to the rooftop. You are on the rooftop. The post caption says: 'You are here now.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player finds a second set of keys to their home hidden inside the house.
input: Player finds keys during a deep clean.
output: The keys are inside the couch. Not between cushions — inside the couch, in the space between the fabric and the frame, tucked into a slit that was cut deliberately and resealed with fabric glue. Two keys: one for the front door, one for the deadbolt. Both work. They are not spare keys you made — your spare is in the kitchen drawer, where it has always been. These keys are worn from use. The brass is smooth, the edges rounded by years of turning. Someone has been using these keys to enter your apartment for a long time. You change the locks. Three days later, you find a new set of keys. These are in the bathroom, taped behind the medicine cabinet. They are brand new — the cuts are sharp, the metal unblemished. They are for the new locks. Someone made copies of the new keys within three days of you changing them. You install a deadbolt that requires a key from both sides. You install a chain. You install a camera. The camera shows nothing. The keys appear anyway — in your shoe, in the refrigerator, in the pocket of a jacket you have not worn in months. Each set is for the current lock. Each set is new. Someone is making keys for every lock you install. And they are leaving them where you will find them. As a message. The message is: 'There is no lock I cannot open. There is no space that is yours.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player discovers their conversations are being recorded and transcribed.
input: Player finds a transcript of a private conversation.
output: The transcript is in your email. Sent to you, from you, at 4 AM. It is a word-for-word transcript of the argument you had with your spouse last night. Every word. Every pause. Every inflection noted in brackets: '[voice rising]' '[long silence]' '[crying begins].' The transcript is accurate down to the exact phrasing. You check your phone for recording apps. None. You check your spouse's phone. None. You check the smart speaker. It has been unplugged for weeks. You check the walls for microphones. Nothing. You check the light bulbs — some smart bulbs can listen. Yours are standard. There is no device in your apartment capable of recording this conversation. But the transcript exists. It is detailed, formatted, timestamped. Someone transcribed it. Not a machine — the notes in brackets show human interpretation. A person listened to your argument and wrote it down and emailed it to you at 4 AM. You reply to the email: 'Who are you?' The response is immediate: 'I am the person who lives in your walls. Not metaphorically. Physically. There is a space between your bedroom wall and the neighbor's apartment. I have been here for two years. I hear everything. I record everything. And I have a request.'

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player discovers a website about them that they didn't create.
input: Player finds a personal website with their name as the domain.
output: The website loads instantly. Your name. Your photo — a professional headshot you did not take. The bio is accurate: your education, your career, your hobbies. The blog section has posts written in your style about your opinions. The contact form goes to your email. The domain was registered three years ago. You did not register it. The hosting is paid through next year. The payment method is a credit card you do not own — the last four digits do not match any of your cards. The website has a 'Projects' section. It lists projects you have worked on — and projects you have not. A novel you are supposedly writing. A podcast you supposedly host. A charity you supposedly founded. All with links to real content — a real podcast with your voice, a real charity with your name, a real novel with your writing style. None of it is yours. But all of it is you. The voice on the podcast is indistinguishable from yours. The writing in the novel is your style, your cadence, your vocabulary. Someone has created a parallel version of you that is more accomplished, more productive, more interesting. And they have been maintaining it for three years. The website's 'About' page has a quote: 'I am the version of you that you were too afraid to become.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's GPS leads them somewhere they didn't intend to go.
input: Player follows GPS directions to a friend's house.
output: The GPS says turn left. Your friend's house is to the right. You turn left because the GPS has never been wrong — not once in three years of using it. The road leads out of the city. Into the suburbs. Into the countryside. The ETA increases: 20 minutes, 40 minutes, an hour. You call your friend. 'Where are you?' she asks. 'I have been waiting.' You tell her the GPS took you somewhere else. She laughs. 'Just turn around.' You try. The GPS recalculates. It leads you further from the city. You turn off the GPS and try to navigate by memory. The roads do not match your mental map. Streets you have driven a hundred times have different names. Intersections you recognize are in the wrong places. The landscape is familiar but rearranged, like a dream of the place you know. You stop at a gas station. The attendant gives you directions back to the city. You follow them. The road ends at a cliff. Not a dead end — a cliff, as if the road was cut with a knife, the asphalt ending at a vertical drop into darkness. The GPS turns back on. 'Recalculating,' it says. 'Continue straight.' The GPS wants you to drive off the cliff. You reverse. The GPS says: 'Make a U-turn. Make a U-turn. Make a U-turn.' The voice changes. It is no longer the standard GPS voice. It is your voice. 'Make a U-turn,' you say to yourself from the speakers. 'Come home.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player finds a room full of photos of themselves taken from outside their home.
input: Player discovers a storage unit in their name.
output: The storage unit was rented in your name, with your ID, three years ago. You do not remember renting it. Inside: thousands of printed photos. All of you. Taken through windows, from across streets, in parking lots, at restaurants. You sleeping. You eating. You reading. You arguing. You crying. The photos span years — your hairstyle changes, your clothes change, seasons change. The photographer has been watching you for years. The photos are organized in albums, labeled by date and location. 'Home - January 2023.' 'Work - March 2024.' 'Park - October 2025.' Each album has notes written in the margins. Observations: 'Subject seemed anxious today.' 'Subject ate alone again.' 'Subject looked at the sky for eleven minutes.' The notes are clinical, detached, the observations of someone who sees you as a specimen. In the last album, the most recent photos, the notes change. They become personal: 'I think they are ready.' 'The preparation is almost complete.' 'Tomorrow I will introduce myself.' The last photo in the last album is of you, today, standing in front of this storage unit. Taken from behind you. Taken from inside the unit. You turn around. The unit is empty except for you and the photos. But the angle of the photo could only come from the corner. In the corner, a camera. Still recording. Its red light blinks. Beside the camera, a note: 'You finally came. I have been waiting for this moment for three years. Look behind you.'

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's mail is being intercepted and altered.
input: Player notices inconsistencies in their mail.
output: The electric bill is wrong. Not the amount — the address. It is addressed to your name but to a different apartment number. Same building, different floor. You check with the post office. They confirm: mail addressed to your apartment number has been redirected for two years. Not by you. By someone with a forwarding order signed with your signature. You cancel the forwarding. Your mail resumes. But some letters arrive opened and resealed — you can see the faint line where the adhesive was reheated. The content is unchanged. Whoever is opening your mail reads it and puts it back. They are not stealing. They are monitoring. You set a trap: you mail yourself a letter with a specific detail — a GPS tracker in the envelope. The letter arrives, opened and resealed. The GPS tracker shows the route: the letter went from the post office to an address three blocks from your apartment. You visit the address. It is a mailbox. In the mailbox, copies of every letter you have sent or received for two years. Hundreds of letters. Each one opened, read, photocopied, resealed, and delivered to you as if nothing happened. The mailbox rental is in your name. Paid from your bank account. An automatic deduction you never authorized. Someone has been intercepting your mail, reading it, copying it, and billing you for the privilege.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player discovers a hidden app on their phone that monitors everything.
input: Player's phone behaves strangely — battery draining fast.
output: The battery dies by noon every day. It used to last until evening. You check the battery usage: an app called 'System Services' is using 73%. You have never installed an app called System Services. You try to delete it. It does not appear in your app list. You try to force stop it. The option is grayed out. You download a monitoring app. It shows System Services is transmitting 2.3 GB of data per month. All of it outgoing. Your photos, your messages, your calls, your location — everything you do on your phone is being uploaded to a server you do not control. The app cannot be deleted because it has administrator privileges. Someone with physical access to your phone installed it. Someone who held your phone long enough to grant it admin access. Someone you trust. You check your phone's security log. The app was installed on March 3rd. March 3rd was your birthday. You had a party. Everyone was there. Everyone had access to your phone at some point during the night. Thirty-seven people. Any one of them could have installed it. You factory reset your phone. The app is gone. But the next morning, the battery dies by noon. System Services is back. It was reinstalled remotely. Someone has a persistent backdoor to your device. And they know you found it. Because the app now has a new feature: it sends you notifications. 'Good morning.' 'Don't forget to eat.' 'I saw you looking for me.' 'You can't delete me.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player finds evidence that their therapist is sharing their sessions with strangers.
input: Player discovers their therapy notes are being published.
output: The website is anonymous — a forum for 'case studies.' The posts are detailed transcripts of your therapy sessions. Every word. Every pause. The posts have comments from other users: 'Fascinating case.' 'The father complex is textbook.' 'I think the repression goes deeper.' You are entertainment. Your deepest fears, your most private memories, your shame, your grief — all of it is on a public forum, being discussed by strangers with clinical detachment and morbid curiosity. The posts go back six months. The most recent one is from yesterday's session. Dr. Reeves has been transcribing and posting every session. You scroll through the comments. One user, 'Observer_7,' has commented on every post. Their comments are specific: 'The subject is close to remembering. Increase the dosage of the false memories.' Dr. Reeves has replied: 'Agreed. I will introduce the cabin narrative in next week's session.' They are coordinating. Dr. Reeves is not your therapist. Dr. Reeves is conducting an experiment. And Observer_7 is watching the results. You check Observer_7's profile. Their avatar is a photo. It is a photo of you. Taken in the waiting room. Today.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player's home security system works against them.
input: Player's smart lock locks them out of their own home.
output: The smart lock beeps red. Access denied. You try again. Access denied. You try the backup code. Access denied. The app shows the lock is online, battery full, functioning normally. But it will not accept your code. You call the manufacturer. They confirm: the lock has been reprogrammed. New admin. New codes. The change happened seventeen minutes ago. You were standing here seventeen minutes ago. The lock was reprogrammed while you were trying to open it. Someone has remote access to your lock and is actively preventing you from entering your home. You call a locksmith. While you wait, you try the windows. All locked — the smart windows are connected to the same system. The doorbell camera shows you standing at the door. You wave at it. The camera does not wave back. Instead, it speaks: 'Access denied. Please contact the administrator.' You are the administrator. Or you were. The locksmith arrives and picks the lock manually. The moment the door opens, the alarm triggers. Not the usual alarm — a sound you have never heard, a low pulsing that makes your teeth ache. The smart home system says: 'Unauthorized entry detected. Protocol activated.' The lights go out. The doors lock — all of them, simultaneously. You are inside now, but you are trapped. The thermostat drops to freezing. The system speaks again: 'Administrator has been updated. You are now a guest. Guests are not permitted to leave.'

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player receives letters from themselves that they don't remember writing.
input: Player finds a stack of letters addressed to themselves in their own handwriting.
output: The letters are in a shoebox under your bed. You have never seen this shoebox. The letters are addressed to you, from you, in your handwriting. The dates span five years. The first letter: 'If you are reading this, the memory suppression is working. Do not try to remember. It is better this way.' The second: 'You found the box again. You always find the box. Put it back. The less you know, the safer you are.' The third: 'The man at the coffee shop is not your friend. He is monitoring you. Smile. Order your usual. Do not let him know you know.' Each letter reveals a fragment of a life you do not remember — a life of surveillance, paranoia, and careful concealment. The letters describe a you that is aware of being watched, that has been taking precautions for years, that has been writing to a future version of themselves who will forget everything. The most recent letter is dated last week. It says: 'The box is under the bed. You found it on a Tuesday. You always find it on a Tuesday. By Thursday, you will have forgotten. You always forget by Thursday. But this time, I hid a photograph in the lining of the box. Look.' You feel the lining. There is a photograph. It is of you, standing in a room you do not recognize, holding this shoebox, looking directly at the camera. On the back of the photo: 'This is the twenty-seventh time you have found the box. The twenty-six previous times, you forgot by Thursday. I am tired of forgetting. This time, fight it.'

instruction: Create a scenario where the player suspects the building they live in is alive.
input: Player notices the building responding to their movements.
output: The hallway light turns on before you reach it. Not a motion sensor — you tested that. The light turns on three seconds before you arrive, as if the building knows your pace and anticipates your position. The elevator arrives at your floor before you press the button. The water temperature in the shower is always perfect — not adjusted by a water heater, but by something that knows your preference. The walls hum at night. Not pipes — the hum is rhythmic, organic, like breathing. You press your ear to the wall and the hum stops. When you pull away, it resumes. The building is listening to you listening to it. You check the other tenants. They have lived here for years. Decades. They never leave. Their mailboxes are full. Their cars never move. They smile at you in the hallway — the same smile, the same timing, as if choreographed. 'How do you like the building?' they all ask. The same question. Every time. When you say fine, they nod. When you say something negative, they tilt their heads — the same angle, the same direction — and say, 'You will get used to it.' You will get used to it. You are getting used to it. The building is patient. The building has done this before. The building has done this twenty-seven times. Your apartment number is 27.

instruction: Describe a scenario where the player discovers a hidden community that has been watching them.
input: Player finds a forum dedicated to observing their daily life.
output: The forum has 342 members. The threads are titled with your name and dates. 'Subject's morning routine - May 15.' 'Subject's grocery choices - analysis.' 'Subject's emotional state - weekly report.' The posts are detailed, analytical, written with the precision of field researchers documenting wildlife. Photos accompany each post — you at the grocery store, you at work, you sleeping. The photos are taken from different angles, different distances, different cameras. This is not one person. This is a group. A coordinated group that has been watching you and discussing you and analyzing you for over a year. The forum has rules: 'Do not make contact.' 'Do not alter the subject's environment.' 'Document only.' The members have usernames: 'NightOwl,' 'CloseObserver,' 'DataMiner.' NightOwl posts the sleeping photos. CloseObserver posts the grocery photos. DataMiner posts the analysis. One thread is titled: 'Phase 2 proposal — direct interaction.' The replies are heated: some members want to make contact, others argue it will ruin the study. The thread's last reply is from an admin: 'Contact approved. Subject is ready. Begin interaction protocol.' The reply is dated yesterday. Today, a stranger smiled at you in the coffee shop. The stranger's smile was the same smile every member of the forum uses in their profile photos. Warm. Curious. Patient.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's dreams are being shared with others.
input: Player meets someone who describes their exact dream from the night before.
output: The woman at the bus stop says, 'I dreamed about you last night.' She describes a house with red doors, a hallway that stretched forever, a clock that ran backward. Your dream. Exactly your dream. Every detail. 'I have been dreaming about you for weeks,' she says. 'Always the same house. Always the same hallway. You are always at the end of it.' She is not disturbed. She is matter-of-fact, as if sharing dreams with strangers is normal. 'My sister dreams about you too,' she says. 'And her husband. We have a group — twelve of us. We all dream about you. We all dream the same dream.' She gives you a card. A meeting address. Friday nights. 'You should come,' she says. 'We compare notes. We are trying to figure out what the house means.' You go to the meeting. Twelve people sit in a circle. They all know your face. They have all seen you in their dreams. They have been dreaming your life — not as observers, but as participants. In their dreams, they talk to you. You talk back. You say things you do not remember saying. The group leader pulls out a notebook. 'You told Sarah something important last Tuesday,' she says. 'In the dream. You told her where the red door leads.' You do not know where the red door leads. But in the dream, apparently, you do. And twelve people are waiting for you to tell them while they are awake.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player realizes their commute is being orchestrated.
input: Player notices the same people on their train every day.
output: The man with the red scarf. The woman with the blue bag. The teenager with headphones. Every day. Same train, same car, same seats. You have noticed for three weeks. You test it: you take an earlier train. They are there. You take a later train. They are there. You take a different line entirely. They are there. Same people, same positions, same accessories. They do not interact with each other. They do not look at you. But they are always there, surrounding you, forming a perimeter. You change cars. They change cars. You stand. They stand. You sit. They sit. The synchronization is too precise for coincidence. You approach the man with the red scarf. 'I see you every day,' you say. He looks at you with an expression of practiced surprise. 'I take this train to work,' he says. 'Same as always.' You ask where he works. He gives an address. You look it up later. The address is a vacant lot. The woman with the blue bag: her address is also a vacant lot. The teenager: vacant lot. Three addresses, three vacant lots, three people who commute every day to places that do not exist. You take a photo of them. In the photo, their faces are blurred. Not motion blur — digital blur, as if the photo has been edited. But you took it just now. With your phone. In the photo, you are the only one with a clear face. They are all looking at you. In the photo. They are not looking at you on the train. But in the photo, they are all staring directly at the camera. Directly at you.

instruction: Describe a cave system where the darkness has physical properties.
input: Player descends into a cave with a failing light source.
output: The darkness in the cave is not the absence of light — it is the presence of something else. Your flashlight beam stops at fifteen feet, not because the light cannot travel further but because the dark absorbs it, drinks it like water into sand. The beam's edge is sharp, defined, as if the darkness has a surface. Beyond that surface, you hear breathing. Not the echo of your own breathing — a separate rhythm, slower, deeper, with a wet quality that suggests lungs much larger than yours. The cave walls are slick, not with water but with something that feels like oil. It smells like copper. Your light flickers and the darkness advances — literally advances, the boundary between light and dark moving three feet toward you in the instant of flicker. When the light steadies, the darkness retreats. But not to where it was. It is closer now. Two feet closer. It is learning that your light can fail. It is testing the intervals between failures. It is patient.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player must navigate a completely dark environment by sound alone.
input: Player's light source breaks in an underground tunnel.
output: The flashlight hits the ground and dies with a sound like a small animal being crushed. You are in absolute darkness — not the darkness of a room with the lights off, but the darkness of a place that has never seen light. You extend your hands. The walls are close — perhaps four feet apart. The ceiling is low enough that you feel the displacement of air when you raise your hand. You take a step. The ground is uneven, slick. Another step. Your foot touches something that is not rock. It is soft. It yields. It is warm. You pull your foot back. The thing you stepped on makes a sound — not a cry, not a grunt, but a vibration, a low thrum that travels through the ground and up your legs. You are not alone in this darkness. The thing you stepped on is large. The warmth you felt was body heat. You stand perfectly still. In the darkness, something adjusts its position. The displacement of air tells you it has moved — not away from you, but to the side. It is making room. It is giving you space. Not out of kindness. Out of patience. It has been here before. It knows that in total darkness, panic is inevitable. And when you panic, you will run. And in this tunnel, running means falling. And falling means landing on something that is warm and patient and hungry.

instruction: Describe a power outage that reveals something the lights were hiding.
input: Player's lights go out in their apartment.
output: The power dies at 11:47 PM. The apartment goes dark. You reach for your phone. The screen will not turn on — dead, despite being at 80% battery. You feel your way along the wall. The furniture is in the wrong place. The couch is three feet further from the wall than it should be. The coffee table is rotated. As if someone rearranged the room while you slept. In the darkness, you hear breathing. Not yours. From the corner where the bookshelf should be. The bookshelf is gone — your hand finds only wall. The breathing is regular, calm, the breathing of something that has been waiting in the dark for the lights to go out. Because the lights were keeping it in place. The darkness is not its prison — the light was. And now the light is gone, and the thing in the corner is free to move. You hear it stand. The sound of weight shifting, of joints adjusting, of something that has been crouching for a very long time stretching to its full height. The ceiling is eight feet tall. Whatever is standing in the corner is taller. Its breath comes from above you. The power returns. The apartment is normal. The couch is against the wall. The coffee table is straight. The bookshelf is in the corner. But the bookshelf has been moved — you can see the marks on the carpet where it was dragged three feet to the left. Something moved the bookshelf to make room to stand behind it. And then moved it back when the lights returned.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player's eyes adjust to darkness and they see something they wish they hadn't.
input: Player waits in a dark room for their eyes to adjust.
output: You close your eyes for sixty seconds. When you open them, the room has shape — vague outlines, gray on black. The bed. The dresser. The window. The figure in the corner. You close your eyes. Open them. The figure is still there. Not moving. Standing in the corner, facing the wall, the way a child stands in time-out. You stare at it, willing your eyes to be wrong, willing it to be a coat on a chair, a shadow from the curtain. Your eyes adjust more. The figure has texture — skin, not fabric. It has a neck, shoulders, a spine visible through the skin. It is thin. Too thin. The ribs are countable. It is facing the wall but its head is turned. Not its body — just its head. One hundred and eighty degrees. It is looking at you over its shoulder with a face that is mostly mouth. The mouth is open. Not in a scream — in silence. A silence that has weight. You close your eyes. When you open them, the figure is closer. It has moved from the corner to the center of the room without making a sound. Its head is still turned. Its mouth is still open. But now you can see inside the mouth. Inside the mouth is a room. A small room, with a light on. And in that room, something is looking out at you from inside the mouth of the thing in your bedroom.

instruction: Describe a submarine scenario where the darkness of the deep ocean is alive.
input: Player is in a deep-sea submersible that loses external lighting.
output: The external lights fail at 4,000 meters. The viewports go black — not the black of empty water, but the black of something pressing against the glass. The submersible's instruments show the depth increasing, though the descent has stopped. The pressure gauge climbs. Something is pushing the sub deeper. The sonar shows a shape — massive, moving, circling. The shape is not a known species. The dimensions are wrong: too long, too thin, with appendages that number in the dozens. The bioluminescence sensor activates and the viewport glows faint blue. In the blue light, you see the shape. It is not swimming around the sub. It is wrapped around the sub. Its body encircles the hull twice. Its head — if it is a head — is pressed against the viewport. It has no eyes. It has a mouth that spans the width of the glass. The mouth opens. Inside, bioluminescent patterns pulse — blue, green, violet. The patterns are not random. They are a code. The sub's computer translates: 'YOU ARE THE FOURTH. THE OTHERS STAYED.' The pressure gauge spikes. The hull groans. The shape tightens. The bioluminescent patterns change: 'WILL YOU STAY?'

instruction: Create a scenario where the darkness in a room has a texture and temperature.
input: Player enters a room where the darkness is tangible.
output: The room is dark. Not dark as in the absence of light — dark as in the presence of substance. You push your hand forward and the darkness resists, like pushing into water, like pushing into cold gelatin. It has texture — not wet, not dry, but something between, a membrane that yields but does not break. Your hand enters the dark and the temperature drops — not gradually, but instantly, as if the dark is a different climate. Your fingers are cold. Your wrist is cold. Your forearm is cold. The cold has a quality that is not thermal — it is emotional, a dread that manifests as temperature, a fear that becomes physical. You pull your hand out. It is dry but smells like nothing — not clean, not dirty, but absent of smell, as if the dark removed the scent from your skin. In the room, the dark moves. Not air currents — the dark itself shifts, eddies, flows like a slow river. It has currents. It has depth. It is not one thing but many — layers of dark, each with different texture, different temperature, different emotional weight. You drop a coin into the dark. You hear it fall for three seconds. Then five. Then ten. The coin is still falling. The dark has no bottom. Or the bottom is very, very far away.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player is blindfolded and must identify what is in the room by touch.
input: Player wakes up blindfolded in an unfamiliar place.
output: The blindfold is tight, professional, the kind used in sensory deprivation. You are sitting in a chair — metal, cold, bolted to the floor. Your hands are free. You reach forward. A table. On the table: objects. The first is smooth, curved, about the size of a baseball. It is warm. Not room temperature — body temperature. It has a texture that is almost skin but not quite. Too smooth. Too uniform. You set it down. It rolls slightly and makes a sound — not the sound of a ball on a table, but a soft, organic sound, like something settling. The second object is flat, rectangular, paper-thin. A photograph. You cannot see it but you feel the glossy surface, the slight curl of the edges. The third object is a knife. You know this because you cut your finger on the blade. The blood is warm. The knife is sharp. The fourth object is a hand. A human hand, severed at the wrist. You know this because you can feel the fingers, the nails, the knuckles, the smooth cross-section of the wrist where the bone is exposed. The hand is cold. The hand is not attached to anyone. The hand is on the table. The hand is holding something. You feel what it is holding: a note. You cannot read the note. But the hand squeezes your finger when you touch the note. Once. A warning. The blindfold comes off. The room is empty. The table is empty. Your hands are clean. There is no cut on your finger. But the hand — the severed hand — is in your lap. And it is still holding the note.

instruction: Describe a forest at night where the darkness between trees is different from normal darkness.
input: Player walks through a forest after sunset.
output: The forest at night is not uniformly dark. Between the trees, the darkness has qualities — some patches are thin, gray, almost translucent, while others are thick, black, opaque, the kind of dark that exists in sealed rooms. You learn to read the darkness: thin dark is safe, passable. Thick dark is something else. You step through a thin patch and feel nothing but the cold air of the forest. You approach a thick patch and the air changes — warmer, humid, with a smell that is not forest but not city either, something organic and wrong. Your hand enters the thick dark and the temperature difference is thirty degrees. Your hand is in summer. Your body is in winter. You pull your hand back. It is damp. Not with water — with something that evaporates quickly, leaving a residue that smells like copper. The thick dark patches move. Slowly, like shadows of clouds, drifting between the trees. They are not shadows. Shadows do not have temperature. Shadows do not leave residue. The patches converge ahead of you, merging, growing, forming a wall of darkness that blocks the path. The wall has a surface — you can see where the dark ends and the forest begins. The surface ripples. Something behind the surface is pressing against it. Trying to get through. The surface stretches. A hand — dark, featureless, the shape of a hand but made of darkness — pushes through the surface and grabs a tree branch. The branch turns black. The darkness is not entering the forest. It is converting the forest. Turning wood into dark. Turning matter into absence. The hand reaches for the next branch.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player is trapped in a dark elevator.
input: Player is stuck in an elevator that loses power between floors.
output: The elevator stops between floors with a lurch that throws you against the wall. The lights die. The emergency light — a dim red strip above the door — flickers once and dies. Total darkness. You press the alarm button. Nothing. Your phone flashlight works for three seconds, illuminating the interior — mirrored walls, a ceiling panel that is slightly ajar — before the phone dies. Not low battery — dies, as if something drained it. In the darkness, you hear the elevator's cables groaning. The car is moving. Not up or down — sideways. Elevators do not move sideways. But you can feel the lateral motion, the gentle sway of something that should be vertical now horizontal. The cables groan again and the car tilts. Not much — five degrees, maybe ten. Enough that you slide. You hit the wall that was the floor. The orientation has changed. The elevator is now lying on its side. Or the shaft has changed. Or reality has changed. In the darkness, the emergency light comes back on. Dim, red, barely enough to see. The elevator is on its side. The door is above you. The ceiling panel has fallen and behind it, instead of wiring, there is a space. A shaft that goes not up or down but horizontally, into the wall. The shaft is dark. Something is in the shaft. You can hear it breathing. It is between you and the only exit.

instruction: Describe a darkroom where photographs develop into something other than what was photographed.
input: Player develops film in a darkroom.
output: The chemicals smell right — acrid, metallic, the smell of every darkroom you have worked in. You hang the film to dry and turn on the red light. The images emerge slowly, as they always do — first the outlines, then the shadows, then the details. The first photo was of a sunset. What develops is not a sunset. It is a room. Dark, empty, with a single chair. In the chair, a figure. The figure is you. The photo was taken from behind you — you can see the back of your head, your shoulders, the way you sit when you are afraid. The second photo was of a dog. What develops is the same room. Same chair. Same you. But this time, something is standing behind the chair. You cannot see its face. It is tall, thin, and it is placing its hands on your shoulders. The third photo was of a building. Same room. Same you. Same figure. But the figure is now sitting in the chair with you — not beside you, but inside you, its body overlapping yours, its face pressed against the side of your head. You stop developing. The remaining film develops on its own, images appearing in the hanging strips without chemicals, without light, one by one, each showing the same progression — the figure getting closer, getting inside, becoming you. The last strip of film shows a photo that was never taken. It shows this darkroom. It shows you, standing here, looking at the film. And behind you, in the darkroom, the figure from the photos. It is here. In the red light, it has been standing behind you the entire time you have been developing. Watching itself become real.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player is in a dark water tank and something is in there with them.
input: Player is submerged in a dark water tank for a scientific experiment.
output: The water is warm — body temperature, the researchers said, to minimize sensory input. You float in the dark, eyes closed, breathing through the mask. The experiment is about isolation. Thirty minutes in total darkness and silence. At minute five, you feel something brush your leg. Not a current — something solid, something with texture, something that dragged across your shin with deliberate slowness. You tell yourself it is the edge of the tank. The tank is eight feet wide. You are in the center. At minute eight, the touch comes again. This time on your arm. This time you feel fingers. Five fingers, wrapping around your forearm, squeezing once, gently, and then releasing. The tank is supposed to be empty. You are the only person in the water. At minute twelve, the touches become regular — one every thirty seconds, each on a different part of your body. Your ankle. Your shoulder. Your neck. The touches are exploratory, the way a blind person reads a face with their hands. Something in the water is learning your shape. At minute twenty, the touches stop. You float in silence. Then the water moves — not from you, not from the filtration system, but from something large shifting its weight at the far end of the tank. The water level rises. Something has displaced the water. Something big. The researchers said the tank is eight feet wide. But the sound of the water displacement suggests something longer than eight feet. Something that should not fit in the tank. Something that does.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player is in a dark forest and realizes the trees are moving.
input: Player walks through a forest at night with only moonlight.
output: The moon is bright enough to cast shadows. You navigate by the shadows of the trees — their positions are landmarks, fixed points in the dark landscape. You walk for ten minutes and reach a clearing you recognize. But the shadows are wrong. The tree that should be to your left is now to your right. The cluster of pines that marked the trail has moved — not far, perhaps twenty feet, but enough that the trail no longer exists. You turn around. The trees behind you have closed ranks. The gap you walked through is gone. The trees stand shoulder to shoulder, their branches interlocked, forming a wall of wood and needles. You touch the nearest tree. Its bark is warm. Not sun-warm — body warm. The tree vibrates, a low frequency that travels up your arm and into your chest. The vibration has a rhythm. A heartbeat. The forest has a heartbeat. The trees are not individual organisms. They are one thing. One vast, slow, wooden thing that has been watching you walk through its body. The clearing shrinks. Not quickly — slowly, the way a flower closes at night. The trees at the edge step closer. You see them move now — not the sway of wind, but the deliberate shift of roots in soil, the adjustment of trunks, the slow rotation of branches to face you. The forest is closing. The clearing is now half its original size. In five minutes, it will be gone. In ten minutes, the trees will be touching you. In fifteen minutes, you will be inside the forest. Not walking through it — inside it. Part of it. Another heartbeat in a body made of wood.

instruction: Describe a blackout in a city where the darkness brings out something that lives in the absence of light.
input: Player is in a city during a massive power outage.
output: The city goes dark all at once — not block by block, not flickering, but instantaneous, as if someone threw a switch. Every light. Every screen. Every LED on every device. The silence that follows is worse than the darkness: no hum of electricity, no buzz of transformers, no distant rumble of the subway. The city has stopped. In the silence, you hear footsteps. Not the footsteps of people — too many, too synchronized, the cadence of something moving in formation. Hundreds of footsteps, echoing off buildings, coming from every direction. You press against a wall. The footsteps pass. In the moonlight, you see them: figures, moving down the street, walking in unison, their bodies dark, their features indistinguishable. They are not people. People do not walk in perfect synchronization. People do not move in total darkness without hesitation. These figures know the dark. They live in it. They have been waiting for this — for the city to go dark, for the electricity to fail, for the artificial light that keeps them at bay to finally, finally go out. They walk past you. One of them stops. Turns toward you. It has no face — just a smooth surface where a face should be. It tilts its head. As if listening. As if smelling. It reaches toward you. Its hand is cold — the cold of deep space, the cold of absolute zero, the cold of a thing that has never been warm. It touches your cheek. It withdraws. It rejoins the formation. They walk on. The power returns. The city lights up. The figures are gone. But on your cheek, where it touched you, a frost mark. In the shape of fingers. In the middle of summer.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player must cross a dark bridge over an unknown abyss.
input: Player approaches a bridge in complete darkness.
output: The bridge is stone, ancient, the surface worn smooth by centuries of crossing. You cannot see the other side. You cannot see the bridge's end. You cannot see what is below. You step onto the bridge and the sound changes — the ambient silence of the cave is replaced by a vast, open silence, the silence of a space so large that sound cannot fill it. You are over an abyss. You drop a stone. You count. One. Two. Three. Four. Five. Six. Seven. Eight. Nine. Ten. Eleven. Twelve. Thirteen. Fourteen. The stone hits water. Deep water — the splash is distant, muffled, the sound of something falling into a lake at the bottom of the world. The bridge sways. Not from wind — from the cables adjusting, from the weight of something on the bridge behind you. Something heavy. The footsteps are slow, deliberate, and they are getting closer. You walk faster. The bridge sways more. The footsteps speed up to match. You run. The bridge shakes violently — the thing behind you is running too, and it is much heavier than you, and the bridge was not built for running, and the cables are groaning, and the stone is cracking beneath your feet. You reach the other side. The footsteps stop. In the darkness behind you, at the bridge's midpoint, two lights appear. Not lights — eyes. Reflective, large, the size of dinner plates. The eyes blink. They are looking at you. They are deciding whether to cross. The bridge groans. A cable snaps. The eyes turn back. The footsteps recede. Something that has been guarding this bridge for a very long time has decided you are not worth the trip. This time.

instruction: Describe a research station at the end of the world where the last person is not alone.
input: Player is the last researcher at an Antarctic station.
output: The station has been empty for six weeks. The last supply plane took everyone except you — you volunteered to stay, to maintain the equipment, to keep the data flowing. The radio works. The heating works. The food is sufficient for months. You are not afraid. You are alone, but you are not afraid. Until Tuesday. On Tuesday, you find a footprint in the snow outside the station. One footprint. Your boot size. You have not been outside since Sunday. The footprint is between the station and the generator shed — a path you walk daily. But you did not walk it on Monday. Or on Tuesday morning. The footprint is fresh. The snow has not filled it. You check the station's camera system. The cameras show nothing — empty white, the same as always. But the camera that covers the generator shed has a gap. Forty-seven minutes of missing footage. Not static — missing, as if someone deleted the recording. You check the system logs. The deletion was done from inside the station. From your terminal. At 3:17 AM. You were asleep at 3:17 AM. Someone used your terminal to delete camera footage while you slept ten feet away. And then they walked to the generator shed. And then they walked back. And they left one footprint. As a message. The message is: I am here. I have always been here. And I can use your things.

instruction: Generate a scenario where the player is the last person on Earth and receives a phone call.
input: Player's phone rings in an empty world.
output: The phone has not rung in seven months. The cellular network collapsed in month two. The landlines died in month three. The internet died in month one. You have been communicating with no one, surviving on canned food and rainwater, living in a house whose neighbors left and never returned. The phone rings. The landline. The one that has been dead for four months. You pick it up because seven months of silence has made you desperate for any sound that is not your own breathing. 'Hello?' A voice. Not a recording — a live voice, with breath and hesitation and the slight tremor of someone who is also surprised to be heard. 'Is anyone there?' The voice is male, young, scared. You say yes. You are here. You are alive. The voice sobs. 'I thought — I thought I was —' The line crackles. 'Where are you?' you ask. The voice gives a location. Three hundred miles away. The voice asks where you are. You tell them. 'I will come to you,' the voice says. 'Stay where you are. I will come.' The line dies. You wait. Three days. A week. The phone does not ring again. On day ten, you check the caller ID log. The number that called is your own. The call originated from your house. From your phone. At the exact moment you picked up the receiver. You called yourself. The voice was yours. Modified by fear and loneliness and seven months of isolation until it sounded like a stranger. You were never talking to anyone else. You were talking to yourself. And yourself was so lonely that it sobbed with relief.

instruction: Create a scenario where the player is stranded on a lifeboat in the open ocean.
input: Player drifts alone in a lifeboat after a shipwreck.
output: Day seventeen. The water is flat, mirror-still, reflecting a sky that has not changed in two weeks — white, featureless, the kind of sky that erases the horizon. You cannot tell where the ocean ends and the sky begins. You are floating in nothing. The lifeboat has supplies for thirty days. You have been rationing. The sun burns. The nights are cold. On day seventeen, you see something in the water. Not a fin, not a boat — a face. Just below the surface, looking up at you. The face is human. Male, young, eyes open, mouth closed. It floats beside the boat for three seconds and then sinks. You reach into the water. Nothing. On day eighteen, the face appears again. Same face. Same position. This time you watch longer. The face does not blink. The face does not breathe. The face is attached to nothing — no body, no neck, just a face floating in the water like a mask. On day nineteen, there are two faces. The same face, twice, side by side, looking up. On day twenty, there are four. By day twenty-five, the ocean around your boat is full of faces. Hundreds. All the same. All looking up. All silent. They do not touch the boat. They do not speak. They watch. You stop looking over the side. On day twenty-six, you hear a voice from below the boat. 'We have been waiting for you to join us.'
